records.med: SO medium (SN recipe omitting NaNO3, 75% Sargasso seawater base diluted with MilliQ)
records.cite: Zhu et al. Characterization of phytoplankton-excreted metabolites mediating carbon flux through the surface ocean. Proceedings of the National Academy
records.snip: Crocosphaera watsonii WH8501 ... SO ... 27 ... 55 (14:10) ... Exponential phase growth rate (d-1) 0.24
records.notes: Table 1 reports exponential-phase growth rate = 0.24 d-1 directly (RFU-based, batch tube culture). Converted to per-hour: mu = 0.24/24 = 0.01 h-1; doubling_time_h = ln2/0.01 = 69.31 h. Culture inoculated into 25 mL borosilicate tubes, 14:10 h light:dark cycle, 55 umol photons m-2 s-1, monitored via relative fluorescence units (RFU).

records.med: Pro99 (Sargasso seawater base, Moore et al. recipe)
records.cite: Zhu et al. Characterization of phytoplankton-excreted metabolites mediating carbon flux through the surface ocean. Proceedings of the National Academy
records.snip: Prochlorococcus marinus MIT9301 ... Pro99 ... 24 ... 90 (13:11) ... Exponential phase growth rate (d-1) 0.52
records.notes: Table 1 reports exponential-phase growth rate = 0.52 d-1 directly, calculated by the authors from daily cell-count data (axenic culture in 35 mL Pro99 medium, 13:11 h light:dark, 90 umol photons m-2 s-1). Converted to per-hour: mu = 0.52/24 = 0.02167 h-1; doubling_time_h = ln2/0.02167 = 31.99 h.

records.med: SN medium (Waterbury et al. recipe), coastal seawater base
records.cite: Zhu et al. Characterization of phytoplankton-excreted metabolites mediating carbon flux through the surface ocean. Proceedings of the National Academy
records.snip: Synechococcus WH8102 ... SN ... 23 ... 45 (14:10) ... Exponential phase growth rate (d-1) 0.54
records.notes: Table 1 reports exponential-phase growth rate = 0.54 d-1 directly (RFU-based, batch tube culture, 14:10 h light:dark, 45 umol photons m-2 s-1). Converted to per-hour: mu = 0.54/24 = 0.0225 h-1; doubling_time_h = ln2/0.0225 = 30.81 h.

records.cite: Zhu et al. A fitness trade-off between growth and survival governed by Spo0A-mediated proteome allocation constraints in Bacillus subtilis. Science Ad
records.snip: ...is sufficient to account for the faster growth of spo0A-null strain (0.69/hour) than wild-type strain (0.41/hour) here (fig. S5)... The two strains were growing in mannose minimal medium (Fig. 2 caption).
records.notes: Exponential growth rate 0.41/hour reported directly in text for WT strain growing in mannose minimal medium (medium used for the paired proteomics experiment, Fig. 2 caption states 'growing in mannose minimal medium'). doubling_time_h = ln2/0.41 = 1.69 h (calculated). Growth measured via OD600 (five to eight points, OD600 0.05-0.7) on Genesys 50 spectrophotometer, 37C air-bath shaker 200 rpm (aerobic).

records.cite: Zhu et al. A fitness trade-off between growth and survival governed by Spo0A-mediated proteome allocation constraints in Bacillus subtilis. Science Ad
records.snip: ...the faster growth of spo0A-null strain (0.69/hour) than wild-type strain (0.41/hour) here (fig. S5)... The two strains were growing in mannose minimal medium (Fig. 2 caption).
records.notes: Exponential growth rate 0.69/hour reported directly in text for spo0A-null strain in mannose minimal medium (matched proteomics condition, Fig. 2 caption). doubling_time_h = ln2/0.69 = 1.00 h (calculated). Same measurement method as WT record (OD600 exponential-range slope, 37C aerobic shaking).

records.strain: MG1655 derivative, eEGM (multiplex holA/ftsB/dfp base-editing biocontainment system), uninduced
records.cite: Cho et al. Multiplexed CRISPR base editing enables pulse-activated irreversible biocontainment of engineered bacteria. Nucleic Acids Research (2026).
records.snip: the eEGM-bearing strains exhibited growth kinetics (mu_max, 1.65 h-1; doubling time, 0.42 h) comparable to the control group (mu_max, 1.47 h-1; doubling time, 0.47 h) in the absence of inducers
records.notes: mu_max and doubling time reported directly by the authors for the multiplexed eEGM strain (uninduced, no fitness cost condition). Culture in LB medium, 37C, 250 rpm shaking (aerobic), E. coli MG1655 background. Internal consistency check: ln2/1.65=0.420 h, matches reported 0.42 h.

records.cite: Cho et al. Multiplexed CRISPR base editing enables pulse-activated irreversible biocontainment of engineered bacteria. Nucleic Acids Research (2026).
records.snip: ...comparable to the control group (mu_max, 1.47 h-1; doubling time, 0.47 h) in the absence of inducers (P > .05)
records.notes: mu_max and doubling time reported directly by the authors for the control strain (uninduced). Culture in LB medium, 37C, 250 rpm shaking (aerobic), E. coli MG1655 background. Internal consistency check: ln2/1.47=0.4714 h, matches reported 0.47 h.

records.cite: Garcia et al. Proteome trait regulation of marine Synechococcus elemental stoichiometry under global change. The ISME Journal (2024). DOI:10.1093/isme
records.snip: We measured our fixed dilution rate across treatments at 0.178 +/- 0.004 (mean +/- SD) day-1, which we controlled to isolate effects of nutrient and temperature stress from growth rate effects...factorial chemostat desig
records.notes: At chemostat steady state, mu = dilution rate D. Paper reports fixed D = 0.178 +/- 0.004 day-1 across all treatments. Converted to per hour: 0.178/24 = 0.00742 h-1; doubling_time_h = ln2/0.00742 = 93.4 h. This entry represents the P-stress (N:P=80) arm of the 2(nutrient)x3(temperature) factorial chemostat design; same D applied at 20, 24, and 28C to isolate biochemical effects from growth-rate effects.

records.cite: Garcia et al. Proteome trait regulation of marine Synechococcus elemental stoichiometry under global change. The ISME Journal (2024). DOI:10.1093/isme
records.snip: We measured our fixed dilution rate across treatments at 0.178 +/- 0.004 (mean +/- SD) day-1...N-stress (N:P input = 1.7:1) at 20C, 24C, and 28C
records.notes: At chemostat steady state, mu = dilution rate D. Paper reports fixed D = 0.178 +/- 0.004 day-1 across all treatments. Converted to per hour: 0.178/24 = 0.00742 h-1; doubling_time_h = ln2/0.00742 = 93.4 h. This entry represents the N-stress (N:P=1.7) arm of the 2(nutrient)x3(temperature) factorial chemostat design; same D applied at 20, 24, and 28C to isolate biochemical effects from growth-rate effects.

records.med: modified Allen mineral base medium + 0.2% w/v tryptone
records.cite: Andersson et al. Global analysis of mRNA stability in the archaeon Sulfolobus. Genome Biology (2006). DOI:10.1186/gb-2006-7-10-r99. [PMC1794556]
records.snip: S. solfataricus DSM1617 and S. acidocaldarius DSM639 ... cultures were grown at 79°C in modified Allen mineral base medium containing 0.2% w/v tryptone... Growth was monitored by optical density (OD) measurements at 600
records.notes: Doubling time (6 h) reported directly in Materials and Methods (exponential-phase batch culture, OD600 monitoring). growth_rate_per_h calculated as mu = ln(2)/td = ln(2)/6 = 0.1155 h^-1. Oxygen status inferred as aerobic (standard for Sulfolobus grown in shaken Erlenmeyer flasks); pH not stated in text.

records.med: modified Allen mineral base medium + 0.2% w/v tryptone
records.cite: Andersson et al. Global analysis of mRNA stability in the archaeon Sulfolobus. Genome Biology (2006). DOI:10.1186/gb-2006-7-10-r99. [PMC1794556]
records.snip: S. solfataricus DSM1617 and S. acidocaldarius DSM639 ... cultures were grown at 79°C in modified Allen mineral base medium containing 0.2% w/v tryptone... doubling times of 3.5 and 6 h were obtained for S. acidocaldarius
records.notes: Doubling time (3.5 h) reported directly in Materials and Methods (exponential-phase batch culture, OD600 monitoring). growth_rate_per_h calculated as mu = ln(2)/td = ln(2)/3.5 = 0.198 h^-1. Oxygen status inferred as aerobic (standard for Sulfolobus grown in shaken Erlenmeyer flasks); pH not stated in text.

records.cite: Levine et al. Quantitative Characteristics of Gene Regulation by Small RNA. PLoS Biology (2007). DOI:10.1371/journal.pbio.0050229. [PMC1994261]
records.snip: cells were grown overnight in minimal media with antibiotics... To determine the growth rate, OD600 was measured every 60 min... the cell doubling rate (μ) was obtained as the slope of a linear fit of log2(OD600) versus
records.notes: Doubling time reported directly as an approximate value (~2 h) for most of the RyhB/RNA-OUT reporter strains grown at 37°C in M63 + 0.5% glucose minimal medium (12-well plate, shaking, aerobic, batch culture until OD600=0.3). growth_rate_per_h calculated as mu = ln(2)/td = ln(2)/2 = 0.3466 h^-1. Value is an approximation across strains, not a single precise per-strain measurement; pH not stated.

records.cite: Greening et al. Diverse hydrogen production and consumption pathways influence methane production in ruminants. The ISME Journal (2019). DOI:10.1038/s
records.snip: Growth yield (OD600) 0.79±0.01; Specific growth rate (h-1) 0.58±0.19 ... Fumarate (mM) -5.5, Succinate (mM) +2.2, Acetate (mM) +21.8, Ethanol (mM) +8.7 [pure culture, 0-12 h]
records.notes: Specific growth rate (mu=0.58 h-1) directly reported in Table 1 of paper (pure culture of R. albus 7, anaerobic modified Balch medium, headspace 20% CO2/80% N2, 37 degC). Uptake/secretion rates are CALCULATED as delta-concentration (Table 1 footnote: change between 0 and 12 h) divided by 12 h; these are volumetric (mM/h) not biomass-specific rates because the paper gives no OD-to-gDW conversion factor for R. albus. Fumarate: 5.5 mM/12h=0.458 mM/h; succinate: 2.2/12=0.183; acetate: 21.8/12=1.817; ethanol: 8.7/12=0.725 mM/h. Headspace H2 also increased +5.3% over 12h (gas-phase %, not concentration, so not converted to a rate here). Medium composition (Table S6) not reproduced in main text.

records.cite: Greening et al. Diverse hydrogen production and consumption pathways influence methane production in ruminants. The ISME Journal (2019). DOI:10.1038/s
records.snip: Growth yield (OD600) 0.36±0.01; Specific growth rate (h-1) 0.33±0.06 ... Fumarate (mM) -46.3, Succinate (mM) +54.6 [pure culture, 0-12 h]
records.notes: Specific growth rate (mu=0.33 h-1) directly reported in Table 1 (pure culture of W. succinogenes DSM 1740, anaerobic modified Balch medium, headspace 20% CO2/80% H2 as electron donor for fumarate respiration, 37 degC). Fumarate uptake and succinate secretion CALCULATED as delta-concentration/12h (Table 1 footnote gives change between 0 and 12h): fumarate 46.3/12=3.858 mM/h; succinate 54.6/12=4.55 mM/h. Volumetric rates, not per-gDW, since no OD-to-biomass conversion given. Headspace H2 dropped -78.4% over 12h (gas-phase percent, not converted to a rate). Medium composition (Table S6) not reproduced in main text.

records.snip: Specific growth rate (h-1) co-culture 0.57±0.34 (Ra)/0.54±0.11 (Ws) ... Fumarate (mM) -43.1, Succinate +55.4, Acetate +32.4, Ethanol +0.3, H2 BDL [co-culture, 0-12 h]
records.notes: R. albus growth rate in co-culture (mu=0.57 h-1) directly reported in Table 1, tracked by qPCR alongside W. succinogenes. Metabolite concentration changes (Table 1, 0-12h) are for the COMBINED co-culture and cannot be attributed solely to R. albus; rates calculated as delta-conc/12h: fumarate 43.1/12=3.592, succinate 55.4/12=4.617, acetate 32.4/12=2.700, ethanol 0.3/12=0.025 mM/h. H2 was below detection limit in co-culture (fully consumed). Same combined-culture metabolite rates also attached to the paired W. succinogenes co-culture record; volumetric (mM/h), not gDW-specific.

records.cite: Fluke et al. The extensive m5C epitranscriptome of Thermococcus kodakarensis is generated by a suite of RNA methyltransferases that support thermophil
records.snip: T. kodakarensis grows over a wide temperature range (~50 to ~98 °C) with a growth optimum of 85 °C resulting in a ~40-min doubling time under ideal conditions.
records.notes: Doubling time (~40 min = 0.667 h) at the 85°C growth optimum is stated directly in the paper's own text as a general characterization of T. kodakarensis (parental strain TS559, grown anaerobically in ASW-YT+agmatine per this paper's Methods), but is given as an approximate figure without SD/replicate count or an explicit citation in that sentence, so treated as reported (not a fitted/calculated value from a specific dataset in this paper) with medium confidence. growth_rate_per_h calculated as mu = ln(2)/0.667h = 1.04 h-1 for convenience; input number used: doubling time = 40 min stated in paper.

records.med: none (obligate intranuclear symbiont; no axenic growth medium, growth is intracellular within host amoeba nucleus)
records.cite: Schulz et al. Life in an unusual intracellular niche: a bacterial symbiont infecting the nucleus of amoebae. The ISME Journal (2014). DOI:10.1038/isme
records.snip: Following invasion of the nucleus, the symbionts start replicating, and after 24 h, two to four bacteria were present per nucleus. Up to 32 bacteria could be found 48 h p.i., suggesting a doubling time of about 8 h at th
records.notes: Doubling time of ~8 h is explicitly stated by the authors, inferred from bacterial counts per host (Hartmannella sp. FS5) nucleus over time (2-4 bacteria/nucleus at 24 h p.i.; up to 32 bacteria/nucleus at 48 h p.i.). growth_rate_per_h = ln(2)/8 = 0.0866 h-1 (calculated from the paper's stated doubling time). This is intracellular, non-axenic growth of an obligate symbiont; the paper explicitly states 'Growth of purified Nucleicultrix was also not observed under host-free conditions using a variety of standard laboratory media', so no medium composition or defined culture conditions exist. Included as the only quantitative growth observation in this paper; low confidence because it is an approximate count-based estimate for an intracellular bacterium, not a standard culture growth curve.

Medium used specifically for this growth-curve figure (Fig. 6A) is not named in the results text; ASW-YT/ASW-AAW are the two media used for routine cultivation elsewhere in Methods, hence medium confidence is reduced to medium rather than high.

records.ox: not stated in this paper's text
records.med: glucose minimal medium (chemostat), as reported for E. coli K-12 MG1655 by Taymaz-Nikerel et al. 2010 and used/recalculated in this paper
records.cite: Orth et al. A comprehensive genome-scale reconstruction of Escherichia coli metabolism—2011. Molecular Systems Biology (2011). DOI:10.1038/msb.2011.65

up.method: reported (linear regression of E. coli K-12 MG1655 chemostat glucose-uptake-rate vs. growth-rate data)

records.snip: Growth-associated and non-growth-associated maintenances values were recalculated based on recent E. coli K-12 MG1655 chemostat data for growth on glucose minimal media (Taymaz-Nikerel et al, 2010)... The NGAM of 3.15 mm
records.notes: This paper explicitly reports (for iJO1366 model parameterization) that E. coli K-12 MG1655 chemostat data on glucose minimal medium (from Taymaz-Nikerel et al. 2010) gave a linear regression of glucose uptake rate (mmol gDW-1 h-1) vs. growth rate mu (h-1) with slope = 10.83 mmol glucose gDW-1 h-1 per unit mu and intercept = 0.17 mmol glucose gDW-1 h-1 (uptake rate at mu=0, i.e., pure maintenance uptake); NGAM = 3.15 mmol ATP gDW-1 h-1 (=intercept x number of ATP per glucose at P/O=1.375) and GAM = 53.95 mmol ATP gDW-1 were derived by FBA from this same regression. No single discrete growth rate/dilution rate value or explicit medium recipe/temperature/pH/aerobic-anaerobic status is given in this paper's own text for the underlying chemostat experiments (only the fitted regression parameters are reported here), so growth_rate_per_h/doubling_time_h/temperature/pH/oxygen are left null; only the explicitly-stated regression relationship for glucose uptake is captured as an uptake_rate. No product secretion rate is reported/calculable from the numbers given in this paper.

records.snip: It grew optimally at a seawater salinity of 34 and in salinities as low as 11.6. Its maximum growth rate was 0.02 ± 0.007 divisions per hour at 24°C.
records.notes: Value taken verbatim from text: maximum growth rate 0.02 ± 0.007 'divisions per hour' at 24°C in JW2 medium (isolation/artificial seawater medium, composition not detailed in main text). Recorded rate as-is without converting units; note the paper's unit 'divisions per hour' could denote doublings/h rather than a natural-log-based specific growth rate (mu), so doubling_time_h was NOT back-calculated to avoid introducing an unstated assumption. General paper statement: SAR11/related bacterioplankton are aerobic chemoorganoheterotrophs (oxygen condition inferred from this general statement, not stated specifically for this culture).

records.notes: Growth rate reported directly in text as 0.114 d^-1; converted units only (0.114/24 = 0.00475 h^-1). Doubling time reported as ~6 days, converted to hours (6*24=144 h); cross-check: mu=ln2/144h=0.00481 h^-1, consistent with the reported 0.114 d^-1 (0.114/24=0.00475 h^-1). Cultures grown at 23°C, 20 µmol m-2 s-1 light, 12h:12h light:dark cycle, ASN medium (modified ASN-III per Rippka 1988); 3 mM 13C-bicarbonate was an isotope-labeling supplement used to generate labeled biomass, not part of baseline growth medium composition (noted separately).

records.cite: Gao et al. Biological valorization of methane and nitrogen gas-derived ammonia via methanotrophic bacteria for gut-beneficial nutrients. Nature Commun

up.method: reported (peak volumetric methane uptake rate, CUR, ammonia-fed process)

calculated: qCH4 = mu / Yxs; Yxs = 0.97 g DCW/g CH4 (=DCW 5.30 g/L ÷ cumulative CH4 consumption 5.47 g/L, paper's Eq.1); qCH4 = 0.156 h-1 x (1/0.97 g CH4/g DCW) = 0.161 g CH4/gDCW/h; /16.04 g/mol x1000 = 10.0 mmol CH4/gDW/h

calculated: qN = mu x (NH3-N consumed per gDCW); paper states 85.8 mg NH3-N consumed per g cell protein (=1/Yxn, Yxn=11.65 g DCW/g N, Eq.2); qN = 0.156 h-1 x 0.0858 g N/gDCW = 0.0134 g N/gDCW/h; /14.01 g/mol x1000 = 0.956 mmol N/gDW/h
calculated: qO2 = mu x (O2 consumed per gDCW); paper states 98.1 mmol O2 consumed per g cell protein produced; qO2 = 0.156 h-1 x 98.1 mmol O2/gDCW = 15.30 mmol O2/gDW/h

sec.method: reported (volumetric cell protein productivity, 3-L fed-batch, ammonia-fed process)
calculated: qP = mu x protein content; protein content = 347 mg protein/g DCW (Fig.3d); qP = 0.156 h-1 x 347 mg/gDCW = 54.1 mg protein/gDW/h

records.snip: rapid cell growth was observed after 12 h (Fig. 3c), with a maximum specific growth rate of 0.156 h-1 achieved (Supplementary Table 2)... The dry cell weight (DCW) and cell protein productivity reached 5.30 g L-1 and 147
records.notes: mu_max (0.156 h-1) is the reported maximum specific growth rate from the 3-L fed-batch fermentation of engineered strain SO01 on ammonia (main text, Results). doubling_time_h = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.156 = 4.44 h (calculated, not stated verbatim). Yields (Yxs=0.97 gDCW/gCH4, Yxn=11.65 gDCW/gN, 98.1 mmol O2/g DCW, 347 mg protein/g DCW) are overall-run averages from the same fed-batch experiment (Fig.3d, Supplementary Tables 3-4, paper Eqs 1-2: yield = DCW / cumulative substrate consumption). Specific uptake/secretion rates (qCH4, qN, qO2, qP) were calculated as qS = mu / Yxs (or qP = mu x Yp/x) combining the reported instantaneous mu_max with the whole-run average yield; this is an approximation the paper itself does not perform, flagged medium confidence. CUR peak (33.60 mmol/L/h) is a directly reported volumetric (not biomass-normalized) rate, method=reported.

Only the directly reported peak volumetric methane uptake rate (CUR = 42.37 mmol/L/h) is captured; final DCW (6.94 g/L), total CH4 consumed (7.42 g/L), and yields (0.94 g DCW/g CH4, 15.25 g DCW/g N) are reported but are yields/totals, not rates, so were not converted to a specific uptake rate absent a paired mu value for this specific run.

records.snip: E. coli W3350 and W3350(λ) were grown in Tryptone medium with aeration, and 12 samples from each culture were taken about every 6 min between OD600 0.1 and 0.6.
records.notes: Approximate exponential growth rate calculated assuming the stated OD600 range (0.1 to 0.6) was spanned by the described sampling scheme (12 samples taken every ~6 min, i.e. an elapsed window of (12-1)x6 = 66 min = 1.1 h). mu = ln(OD_final/OD_initial)/time = ln(0.6/0.1)/1.1 h = ln(6)/1.1 = 1.7918/1.1 = 1.63 h^-1; doubling time = ln2/mu = 0.425 h (25.5 min). Temperature not stated in text (left null). This is an approximation from indirectly-linked numbers (OD range and sampling cadence were not explicitly stated to correspond 1:1), hence low confidence; applies to both W3350 and W3350(λ), which were grown/sampled in parallel for the microarray time course and not reported as differing significantly in this rich-medium condition.

Doubling time Dt=38-42 min reported directly (converted to h: 0.63-0.70 h). Per-strain individual WT/EP numeric growth rates were only shown in a bar-plot figure (Fig. 1b), not recoverable as text values, so only the aggregate reported range is captured. records.cite: Alpers et al. Replication-associated gene dosage effects shape the genomes of fast-growing bacteria but only for transcription and translation genes.

Medium confidence because this phase is described as decelerating/non-pure-exponential growth (transition toward non-overlapping cell cycles), so a single mu value is a coarser approximation than for the initial exponential phase.

Asp consumption rate γ=0.51±0.05 µM/OD600/s is explicitly reported as the fitted maximum consumption rate parameter from the model (low-density regime fit); kept in reported units because no OD600-to-gDW biomass conversion factor is given anywhere in the paper, so conversion to mmol/gDW/h cannot be computed without inventing an assumption.

records.snip: Standard growth conditions are defined as growth at 65°C and a medium with carbonates for M. marburgensis... TABLE 1 Growth, production, and excretion parameters... MERmean (mmol L-1 h-1)±SD 1.219±0.076... WERmean 2.485±
records.notes: Volumetric CH4/H2O evolution rates (MERmean, WERmean) and total AAER are directly reported in Table 1 for standard growth conditions (65°C, carbonate medium, 50 mL, anaerobic H2/CO2 batch culture, once-daily gassing). Total lipid production rate is already reported per gram dry biomass (specific rate), method=reported. Specific (per-gDW) CH4 and H2O rates were calculated by dividing the volumetric Table-1 rate by an estimated biomass concentration = k(dry-weight calibration factor, Methods, 0.36 g/L per OD578 unit for M. marburgensis) × ODend (Table 1, maximal OD578 reached over the incubation). This assumes ODend approximates biomass concentration at the same time the endpoint MER/WER measurements were taken (both are endpoint/whole-incubation values per Methods), which is an approximation stated explicitly here as a caveat — hence medium rather than high confidence for the two calculated specific rates.

records.snip: The growth kinetics of the cultures, assuming oxygen was the only limiting substrate, were determined by fitting a Monod kinetic model, resulting in a maximum growth rate (mu_max) of 0.11 h-1 (95% CI = 0.09 to 0.12) and
records.notes: mu_max=0.11 h-1 directly reported as the Monod-model asymptotic maximum growth rate (O2 as sole limiting substrate, Km(O2)=1.69 mM), fitted across a batch-culture series with fixed 20% initial CH4 headspace and O2 varied 5%-25% (v/v) in 160-mL serum bottles, 30 C, 200 rpm, NMS + 5 uM CuCl2. Methane/oxygen specific uptake rate ranges are the reported bounds (from linear regression of metabolite vs. biomass concentration over the exponential phase, ~16-40 h) used directly to constrain the GEM; these are aggregate bounds across the same O2-gradient experiment set, not single-condition point values, so they are not individually paired with the 0.11 h-1 mu_max. OD600-to-DCW conversion factor separately reported as gDCW/L = 0.26 x OD600 but not needed for the rates above since these were already reported in gDCW-normalized units. Condition-specific per-experiment growth rates, and yields of biomass (0.021 gDCW/mmol-CH4 max), CO2 (0.39 mmol/mmol-CH4 max), formate (0.025 mmol/mmol-CH4 max) and acetate (0.072 mmol/mmol-CH4 max) are shown only in figures (not as text numbers paired with a matching qS in the main text), so specific secretion rates could not be reliably back-calculated and were not recorded.

records.cite: Leiby et al. Metabolic Erosion Primarily Through Mutation Accumulation, and Not Tradeoffs, Drives Limited Evolution of Substrate Specificity in Escher
records.snip: These have been evolved since 1988 in 50 mL flasks containing 10 mL of DM media with 139 µM glucose (25 mg/L) as a carbon source. The cultures were grown at 37°C while shaking at 120 rpm, and were transferred daily via 1
records.notes: Calculated from the paper's explicitly stated serial-transfer regime: 1:100 daily dilution corresponds to log2(100)=6.64 doublings/day (paper states this figure directly as '~6.64 net doublings per day'). doubling_time_h = 24 h / 6.64 = 3.61 h; growth_rate_per_h (mu) = ln(2)/3.61 = 0.192 h-1. This represents the average NET growth rate over the full 24-h daily serial-transfer cycle (lag + exponential + glucose-exhausted stationary phase), not a continuous single-phase exponential rate, so it is a lower-bound/average approximation of true exponential mu during the glucose-replete portion of the cycle; flagged medium confidence for this caveat. No explicit numeric growth-rate (h-1) values were given elsewhere in the paper for the many strain/substrate combinations profiled in Figures 1-4 (only log-ratio heatmap figures), so those were not extracted.

records.cite: Yan et al. Respiration-driven methanotrophic growth of diverse marine methanogens. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United State
records.snip: The doubling time for ferrihydrite-dependent methanotrophic growth of M. acetivorans C2A was 29 d compared to 12 d for ferrihydrite-dependent acetotrophic growth
records.notes: mu = ln2/Td using reported Td=29 d (696 h) for ferrihydrite-dependent methanotrophic growth, batch culture at 37C, pH 7.2-7.4, anaerobic (N2/CO2 80/20 atmosphere), shaking 100 rpm. Methane uptake rate qS calculated as mu/Yxs, where Yxs (g biomass/mol CH4) = 0.68 C-mol biomass (per mol CH4, from balanced Eq.1 in paper) x 24.997 g/C-mol (biomass MW estimated from the paper's stated empirical formula CH1.77O0.49N0.24). Formate and acetate secretion rates = qS(CH4) x their stoichiometric coefficients from Eq.1 (0.18 and 0.09 mol per mol CH4, respectively). Multi-step calculation with an assumed ash-free biomass MW; flagged low confidence.

records.notes: Fitness-assay growth phase (strains GMI1000, PSI07, IBSBF1503 grown together under identical protocol) in CPG rich medium reported '20 to 24 h; 5 or 6 cell doublings' to reach saturation from OD600=0.02 seed. Calculated using midpoint values (22 h, 5.5 doublings): doubling_time_h = 22/5.5 = 4.0 h; growth_rate_per_h (mu) = ln2/4.0 = 0.1733 h-1. This is an average generation rate across the whole growth-to-saturation window (includes any lag), not a pure exponential-phase mu; full reported range implies doubling_time 3.33-4.8 h (mu 0.144-0.208 h-1). Paper does not distinguish doubling count by strain for CPG, so identical value applies to all three strains (see also PSI07 and IBSBF1503 records).

NO3- reduction rate (=NH4+ production rate, since reduction was stoichiometric/complete) and acetate consumption rate are explicitly reported bulk reactor rates (umol h-1); dry biomass concentration in the chemostat was not reported, so these could NOT be converted to a specific (mmol/gDW/h) rate -- reported as-is with bulk units, not the preferred per-biomass form.

records.notes: qS(H2) = unit conversion of reported whole-cell Vmax(app)=3.76 umol H2 min-1 (mg biomass)-1: 3.76 umol/min/mg x 60 min/h x 1000 mg/g / 1000 (umol->mmol) = 225.6 mmol H2 gDW-1 h-1. records.med: Anaerobic minimal medium with acetate as electron donor and iodate (IO3-) as electron acceptor
records.cite: Reyes-Umana et al. Genetic and phylogenetic analysis of dissimilatory iodate-reducing bacteria identifies potential niches across the world's oceans.
records.snip: The doubling time of cells grown under these conditions is 10.96 h (µ = 0.06) which is roughly three times longer than cells growing under aerobic conditions in analogous media (3.42 h, µ = 0.20).
records.notes: Both mu (0.06 h^-1) and doubling time (10.96 h) directly reported in text for anaerobic acetate/iodate-respiring growth. Medium recipe (per liter salts) taken from Methods 'minimal media' recipe; acetate (10 mM) and iodate (2 mM) concentrations taken from the routine passaging/growth protocol described in Methods (matches the reported optimum of 2 mM IO3-). Substrate consumption was also reported (2.46±0.499 mM IO3- consumed, 2.86±0.427 mM acetate oxidized, OD600 increase of 0.109) but no incubation time is stated in the text, so a specific uptake/secretion rate (per gDW per h) could not be calculated.

Exact aerobic medium composition (e.g., whether iodate/molybdate were omitted) is not restated verbatim at this point in the text, so composition is assumed identical to the base minimal medium recipe minus iodate; confidence downgraded to medium for the composition field only (rate values themselves are explicitly stated).

records.notes: Calculated average specific secretion rate qP = titer / (initial DCW x time). Inputs from text: final acetone titer 17.46 g/L; initial OD600 = 18, converted to DCW using paper's stated factor 1 OD600 = 0.33 g/L DCW -> 5.94 gDCW/L; bioreactor run time 96 h. qP = 17.46 g/L / (5.94 gDCW/L x 96 h) = 0.03062 g/gDCW/h = 30.62 mg/gDCW/h; converted to molar using acetone MW 58.08 g/mol = 0.527 mmol/gDCW/h. Assumes approximately constant biomass over the run because this is a 'resting catalytic' (non-growing) stage per the paper's own iPBRC framework; this is an approximation since exact biomass time-course was not given, hence low confidence.

records.cite: Basan et al. Overflow metabolism in E. coli results from efficient proteome allocation. Nature (2015). DOI:10.1038/nature15765. [PMC4843128]
records.snip: the rate of acetate excretion per biomass, Jac, exhibits a simple, threshold-linear dependence on growth rate... with a linear dependence above a characteristic growth rate (λac ≈ 0.76 h−1, or 55 min per doubling), below
records.notes: Directly reported threshold growth rate (lambda_ac = 0.76 h-1, doubling time 55 min = 0.917 h) at which acetate overflow begins for WT E. coli NCM3722 grown in MOPS minimal batch culture across a variety of glycolytic carbon sources (Fig. 1). Above this growth rate, acetate secretion rate Jac = Sac*(mu-lambda_ac) with stoichiometric slope Sac=1/3 (dimensionless, mmol acetate per mmol carbon flux); no absolute qP in mmol/gDW/h given in main text so secretion_rates left empty.

records.snip: When supplied with 10 g/L glucose, B-768 grew at a rate of 0.59 ± 0.01 h−1... glucose uptake rate of 1.67 ± 0.15 g glucose/OD600/h... a productivity of 1.46 ± 0.03 g lactate/OD600/h, and a yield of 0.89 ± 0.01 g lactate/
records.notes: Growth rate (mu=0.59 h^-1), glucose uptake rate (1.67 g glucose/OD600/h) and lactate productivity (1.46 g lactate/OD600/h) are all directly reported values (bottle batch fermentation, basal medium with 10 g/L glucose, 50C, MES-buffered pH 6.5, 25 mL Pyrex bottles/20 mL medium, 100 rpm). Units are as reported by the paper (per-OD600, not per gDW) because no OD600-to-gDW/L conversion factor is given in this paper, so no unit conversion to mmol/gDW/h was attempted. Lactate yield of 0.89 g lactate/g glucose (~90% of theoretical max) also reported but not converted to a secretion rate beyond the directly-stated productivity value.

records.notes: Growth rate (mu=0.32 h^-1), xylose uptake rate (0.35 g xylose/OD600/h) and lactate productivity (0.07 g lactate/OD600/h) are all directly reported values from the same batch bottle fermentation experiment as the glucose condition (basal medium, ~10 g/L xylose, 50C, pH 6.5 MES buffer). Only 3.52 g/L of ~10 g/L xylose was consumed after ~24 h. Units left as reported (per-OD600) because no OD-to-gDW conversion factor was given in the paper.

records.notes: High-throughput Bioscreen Pro microplate screen (basal medium, initial OD600=0.1, 300 uL wells) across temperatures 30-59C; maximum specific growth rate at the optimal temperature range (45-53C, midpoint ~49C used here) was directly reported as 0.4 h^-1 on glucose and 0.2 h^-1 on xylose/cellobiose. records.snip: Cells were then washed out at a dilution rate of ~0.104 per day, corresponding to a doubling time of 6.65 days. NO was reduced at a rate of 1.69 mmol per day (20.93 µmol per mg protein per day) and formate was consumed a
records.notes: Dilution rate D=0.104/day explicitly reported at steady state after membrane removal (day 331); at chemostat steady state mu=D, so growth_rate_per_h = 0.104/24 = 0.004333 h-1 (consistent with reported doubling time 6.65 d = 159.6 h; ln2/159.6=0.00434, cross-check OK). Specific uptake rates given directly in paper as µmol per mg protein per day for NO (20.93) and formate (29.01); converted to mmol/g protein/h by dividing by 24 (unit identity: µmol/mg = mmol/g). This is a mixed enrichment community, not an axenic culture; rates attributed to whole community biomass, dominated by the two Candidatus Sterolibacteriaceae species.

Secretion rates are volumetric averages calculated from the single reported timepoint (38 h) assuming zero initial lactate/acetate concentration, per formula qP=Δ[P]/Δt; could not be normalized to biomass (gDW/L) since only OD600 was reported.

records.snip: The rate of the methane flux from N. spumigena UHCC 0039 culture to the gaseous environment was determined to be 1.84 nmol h−1 per mg of chlorophyll a, while prevailing chlorophyll a concentration in the culture was 2.4
records.notes: Directly reported specific methane secretion rate for N. spumigena UHCC 0039 growing on methylphosphonic acid (MPn) as sole P source; rate is normalized to chlorophyll a (a biomass proxy for this photoautotroph), not to gDW, and the paper gives no chlorophyll-to-gDW conversion factor so units are left as reported (no unit conversion performed). No numeric growth rate (mu) or doubling time is reported anywhere in this paper for N. spumigena under any condition -- the paper only states qualitatively that 'Maximal growth rates in studied conditions were obtained in the cultures growing in Pi medium,' so growth_rate_per_h/doubling_time_h are left null rather than invented. Cultures were maintained under continuous illumination (photoautotrophic, aerobic); no cultivation temperature or pH is stated in the text.

This is NOT a conventional defined-medium batch/chemostat measurement -- it is single-cell intracellular (endosymbiotic) growth after experimental host-cell injection, hence culture_mode/oxygen/temperature/pH for the growth measurement itself are not stated by the authors (left null/described qualitatively) and confidence is medium. The paper additionally states this intracellular rate is 'similar to in vitro growth rates in liquid medium and on agar' but does not give a numeric in vitro value, so no separate free-living growth-rate record could be made. Routine stock culture (separate from the measured condition) of R. pickettii K-288 was in Nutrient-Broth medium at 28°C in shake flasks (implying aerobic), per Methods, but no rate was reported for that condition.

records.notes: CALCULATED nominal growth rate for the serial-transfer selection protocol used for all 88 evolving populations: 60-fold dilution every ~22 h implies mu = ln(60)/22h = 4.094/22 = 0.186 h^-1; doubling_time = ln2/mu = 3.73 h (paper itself states this equates to 6-7 generations/day, consistent with log2(60)=5.9 generations/22h). Low confidence: this is the protocol's designed/nominal rate assuming full growth back to the pre-dilution density each cycle in the presence of near-MIC antibiotic ('if new mutants do not appear'), not a directly measured single-strain growth rate, and is far slower than the drug-free doubling times (48-71 min) reported elsewhere in the same paper because cells here are growing under antibiotic stress near their MIC.

records.snip: C growth rates based on C fixation were on average 0.20 d-1, and N growth rates based on N2 fixation 0.03 d-1 during the day...supporting specific growth rates of 0.10 and 0.02 d-1, respectively (considering a 12-h light
records.notes: growth_rate_per_h taken from paper-reported diel-averaged 'specific growth rate' of 0.10 d-1 (C-fixation basis, single-cell SIMS measurements, considering the 12h-light/12h-dark cycle); converted 0.10 d-1 / 24 = 0.0041667 h-1; doubling_time_h = ln2/mu = 166.4 h. Paper also separately reports a day-time-only C-fixation growth rate of 0.20 ± 0.03 d-1 and an N2-fixation-based day-time growth rate of 0.03 ± 0.02 d-1 (Table 3, single-cell SIMS), plus a diel-averaged N-based growth rate of 0.02 d-1 (=0.02/24=0.00083 h-1) -- these alternates are reported here as context, not as the chosen growth_rate_per_h. Uptake/secretion 'biomass-normalized' entries were calculated by dividing the bulk isotope-tracer flux (pmol colony-1 h-1, Table 3) by the colony POC or PON content (Table 2, same paper, mean of n=23 filtered colonies) -- these colonies are not the identical colonies used for the flux measurement, so this is an approximation using paper-internal averages, not a true gDW-normalized rate (no dry-weight or POC/PON value was reported for the same incubated colonies).

records.snip: DIN concentrations were substantially enhanced by 55-94% after 15N-isotope additions...all rates (except for N2/C fixation and ammonium release) should be considered as potential rates, and moreover as net rates due to c
records.notes: These rates come from incubations #3/#4 spiked with ~0.9 µM 15N-nitrate or nitrite, well above typical in-situ oligotrophic-gyre DIN concentrations, added specifically to overcome diffusion-limited solute transport into colonies. The authors explicitly caveat these as POTENTIAL and NET rates (not necessarily representative of unamended in-situ rates); no growth rate is associated with this substrate-amended incubation. No biomass (POC/PON) normalization attempted here because these fluxes reflect an experimentally elevated substrate pool rather than steady-state metabolism tied to the colony biomass values in Table 2.

up.method: calculated: 95% of 0.05 g/100 mL (=0.5 g/L =8.325 mmol/L using MW urea 60.06 g/mol) urea hydrolyzed over 24 h incubation = 7.909 mmol/L consumed / 24 h = 0.3295 mmol/L/h

records.snip: Hydrolysis of the urea added to the culture medium (0.05 g/100 ml) by the isolates ranged from 2 to 95%, with strain S99 (P. penneri) having the greatest...urea hydrolysis. All isolates with urease genes were cultured an
records.notes: Strain S99 had the single highest urea hydrolysis fraction reported in text (95% of added urea). Rate calculated from: urea initial conc. 0.05 g/100mL = 0.5 g/L; MW urea=60.06 g/mol -> 8.325 mmol/L; 95% hydrolyzed over the stated 24 h batch incubation (39C, anaerobic, Methods section 'Ureolytic activity...') = 7.909 mmol/L / 24h = 0.3295 mmol/L/h. This is a volumetric batch consumption rate over the whole 10 mL culture, NOT normalized to biomass/gDW -- the paper only reports urea hydrolysis and ammonia-N production normalized per culture OD600 (arbitrary units, Supplementary Fig. 6), not per gDW, so a true specific qS could not be computed from the numbers given in text. No growth rate (mu) is reported or calculable since only an endpoint OD600 (after 24 h) was measured, not a growth time-course.

records.notes: Cells routinely cultivated at 30°C in steady-state chemostat (N- and energy-limited) at reported dilution rate D=0.015 h^-1; at chemostat steady state mu=D (reported directly, not independently measured for the batch phase). These chemostat-grown cells were then harvested/washed and resuspended into batch experimental medium (pH adjusted to 7.8) for the NOx/N2O measurements reported here, so growth_rate_per_h (0.015) reflects the chemostat seed culture, not necessarily the batch experiment growth phase. Secretion values are peak/accumulated concentrations in ppm (gas headspace) or uM (aqueous), not normalized to biomass (gDW) because reactor headspace volume and biomass density (only OD600=0.2 given) were not reported, precluding conversion to mmol/gDW/h.

records.med: M63 minimal medium + 0.1% glucose + 10 nM PQQ
records.cite: Munder et al. High-affinity PQQ import is widespread in Gram-negative bacteria. Science Advances (2025). DOI:10.1126/sciadv.adr2753. [PMC12124388]
records.snip: in the presence of 10 nM PQQ, the strain grew to a final optical density (OD) of ~0.8, with a doubling rate of ~4 hours.
records.notes: growth_rate_per_h = ln(2)/4 h = 0.1733 h^-1, calculated from the reported doubling rate of '~4 hours' (approximate value, hence medium confidence). Strain is E. coli BW25113 ΔptsΔpqqU phosphotransferase-system-deficient, PqqU-deficient strain complemented with wild-type PqqU on an arabinose-inducible plasmid; growth requires PqqU-mediated PQQ import to activate the PQQ-dependent glucose dehydrogenase Gcd for growth on glucose. M63 medium composition and pH 7 from Materials and Methods. Temperature 37°C is from the general 'Bacterial strains and growth conditions' section (not explicitly restated for this specific complementation growth assay) so is a plausible but not 100% certain assumption; aerobic inferred from shaking batch culture (not explicitly labeled 'aerobic' in text).

records.cite: Baby et al. Inferring the Minimal Genome of Mesoplasma florum by Comparative Genomics and Transposon Mutagenesis. mSystems (2018). DOI:10.1128/mSystem
records.snip: Unlike many other members of the class Mollicutes, M. florum shows a short doubling time of <40 min, requires no sterol for growth, and has no known pathogenic potential.
records.notes: Doubling time stated as '<40 min' is a general background characterization of the species (likely citing the original 1984 isolation report), not a result newly measured in this study; exact value is unspecified beyond the '<40 min' upper bound, so doubling_time_h=0.667 (40 min) is used as an upper-bound placeholder and growth_rate_per_h=ln(2)/0.667=1.04 h^-1 as the corresponding lower-bound estimate (true mu could be higher if Td is shorter than 40 min). This paper's own culture conditions for M. florum L1 (ATCC 1161 medium, 34°C, stated in Materials and Methods: 'M. florum strains were grown at 34°C in ATCC 1161 medium') are reported but not explicitly tied back to a directly remeasured doubling time figure in this paper; low confidence given the mismatch between the background claim and this paper's own measurements (transposon essentiality data), which itself provides no numeric growth-rate re-measurement.

records.snip: bacterial replication in media containing beta-Asp-Arg was confirmed by a marked increase in viable cell counts from 1.9x10^7 CFU/mL to 5.7x10^8 CFU/mL in 48 h of flask growth
records.notes: growth_rate_per_h = ln(N2/N1)/t = ln(5.7e8/1.9e7)/48 h = ln(30)/48 = 0.07086 h^-1; doubling_time_h = ln(2)/mu (calculated) using CFU/mL counts at t=0 (1.9x10^7) and t=48h (5.7x10^8) for P. aeruginosa PAO1 grown on 10 mM beta-Asp-Arg alone (flask, minimal media). Low confidence: this average rate spans the full 48-h culture (including lag and any non-exponential phase) rather than being restricted to the exponential phase, so it understates the true exponential mu; the paper's own exponential-phase-fit doubling time for the same condition is 36.6 h (mu=0.0189 h^-1, see separate record), which is more reliable and should be preferred.

records.med: JW1 (isolation medium), salinity varied by proportional dilution of major ions
records.cite: Savoie et al. Ecophysiology of the Cosmopolitan OM252 Bacterioplankton (Gammaproteobacteria). mSystems (2021). DOI:10.1128/mSystems.00276-21. [PMC8269
records.snip: LSUCC0096 grew in salinities between 5.79 and 63.5, with a maximum growth rate of 0.23 (±0.01) doublings/h at 11.6 under the proportional scheme.
records.notes: Paper reports maximum growth rate as 0.23 (±0.01) doublings/h at salinity 11.6 (proportional-ion-dilution scheme). Converted to natural-log specific growth rate: mu(h-1) = doublings/h x ln2 = 0.23 x 0.6931 = 0.159 h-1. Doubling time = 1/(doublings/h) = 1/0.23 = 4.35 h. Salinity growth experiments were stated to be conducted 'at room temperature' (exact °C not given); all other medium components (carbon, iron, phosphate, nitrogen, vitamins, trace metals) held constant.

records.notes: Paper reports maximum growth rate 0.36 (±0.06) doublings/h at 35°C in JW1 medium. Converted: mu(h-1) = 0.36 x ln2 = 0.36 x 0.6931 = 0.250 h-1. Doubling time = 1/0.36 = 2.78 h. Temperature range tested in triplicate, nonshaking, using JW1 isolation medium.

Converted: mu(h-1) = 0.20 x ln2 = 0.139 h-1; doubling time = 1/0.20 = 5.0 h. records.med: modified JW1 medium, chemolithoautotrophic (no organic carbon except trace vitamins, 100 uM thiosulfate as sole electron donor, bicarbonate as carbon source/buffer)
records.cite: Savoie et al. Ecophysiology of the Cosmopolitan OM252 Bacterioplankton (Gammaproteobacteria). mSystems (2021). DOI:10.1128/mSystems.00276-21. [PMC8269
records.snip: whereas the experimental replicates had a much slower average growth rate of 0.07 ± 0.01 doublings/h (Fig. S9C).
records.notes: Chemolithoautotrophic growth (thiosulfate as sole electron donor, bicarbonate as carbon source) during 4th of 4 consecutive transfers: 0.07 ± 0.01 doublings/h. Converted: mu(h-1) = 0.07 x ln2 = 0.0485 h-1; doubling time = 1/0.07 = 14.29 h. Demonstrates facultative chemolithoautotrophy; growth ~3x slower than heterotrophic positive control (0.20 doublings/h) despite only ~12% lower Gibbs free energy yield, attributed by authors to costlier biosynthesis from inorganic starting materials.

records.med: SACC (standard amino acid complete medium), 20 amino acids, no glucose
records.cite: Liu et al. Clostridium sporogenes uses reductive Stickland metabolism in the gut to generate ATP and produce circulating metabolites. Nature microbiol
records.snip: When cultured anaerobically in defined medium without carbohydrates, C. sporogenes grows rapidly, achieving a doubling time of ~45 minutes (Fig. 1a).
records.notes: Doubling time of ~45 min (0.75 h) reported directly in text for growth in defined medium (SACC, 20 amino acids, 1 mM each, no glucose) under anaerobic conditions. Converted to specific growth rate: mu(h-1) = ln2 / doubling_time_h = 0.6931 / 0.75 = 0.924 h-1. Cultures maintained anaerobically at 37°C (Coy anaerobic chamber, 3.3% H2/10% CO2/85% N2, per Methods). After 24 h most amino acids are depleted and 15 metabolites accumulate (qualitative; no biomass-normalized (gDW) concentrations given, so specific uptake/secretion rates could not be calculated).

records.med: modified DCB-1 mineral medium with testosterone as sole carbon source
records.cite: Wang et al. Bacterial estrogenesis without oxygen: Wood–Ljungdahl pathway likely contributed to the emergence of estrogens in the biosphere. Proceedin
records.snip: cell density increased from ~2.3 × 10^4 cells/mL ... to 1.3 × 10^7 cells/mL ... over 15 d, with a doubling time of 39.2 h. ... ~2.3 mM testosterone was consumed [over 15 d], yielding 0.9 mM estrogen and 1.4 mM androstand
records.notes: Doubling time (39.2 h) reported directly from cell-density increase (2.3x10^4 to 1.3x10^7 cells/mL) over 15 d of fermentative growth on testosterone (1 mM) in modified DCB-1 mineral medium, 30°C, anaerobic (N2/CO2 80:20, dark). Growth-rate field: mu(h-1) = ln2/39.2 = 0.0177 h-1 (calculated from the reported doubling time). Testosterone uptake rate calculated as volumetric rate only (no cell dry-weight/biomass data given to normalize per gDW): ~2.3 mM testosterone consumed over 15 d (360 h) = 2.3/360 = 0.0064 mM/h; NOT converted to mmol/gDW/h because biomass (gDW/L) was not reported, only cell counts. Products (0.9 mM estrone+estradiol combined, 1.4 mM androstanediol) reported over same 15 d period but likewise not biomass-normalized, so secretion rates were not calculated to avoid an unsupported per-gDW figure.

records.snip: Xylose in (+)None and (+)Xyl was depleted initially at a rate of between 3.0 and 4.0 ΔmM/OD600(avg)/h during the first 2 h postaddition and stayed above 2.0 ΔmM/OD600(avg)/h through most of the exponential growth phase.
records.notes: Specific xylose consumption rate directly reported by the authors, normalized to OD600 (not gDW) as ΔmM xylose / average-OD600 / h, for cultures growing on the base 0.5% xylose CGM medium with no additional sugar (+)None or an additional 0.25% xylose (+)Xyl pulse. Units left as reported (mM/OD600/h) because no OD600-to-gDW conversion factor is given in the paper; not converted to mmol/gDW/h to avoid inventing a conversion factor.

records.cite: de la Cruz Barron et al. Shifts from cooperative to individual-based predation defense determine microbial predator-prey dynamics. The ISME Journal (2
records.snip: m (max. growth rate constant of bacteria) = 2 h^-1 [Table 4, ODE model parameter]; organisms cultivated in wheat grass medium; semi-continuous cultures, dilution rate 0.5 day^-1, 19 °C, 120 rpm.
records.notes: Value of 2 h^-1 is a CALIBRATED ODE model parameter ('Max. growth rate constant of bacteria', Table 4) used to fit the Monod-based semi-continuous predator-prey model to observed P. putida KT2440 abundance data in wheat grass medium; it is NOT a directly measured exponential-phase growth rate from an OD/CFU time course. Reported explicitly in the paper's parameter table so growth_method = reported, but flagged low confidence since it is a fitted maximum rate, not an observed one. doubling_time_h computed as ln(2)/2 = 0.347 h for reference only. Separate qualitative finding (not quantifiable): filamentous isolates grew 5% slower than single-celled competitors in the same wheat grass medium at 19°C (no absolute rate given, so not recorded as a separate entry).

records.cite: Dillon et al. Genome-Wide Biases in the Rate and Molecular Spectrum of Spontaneous Mutations in Vibrio cholerae and Vibrio fischeri. Molecular Biology
records.snip: each of which was propagated for 217 days...Gen. per Line 5,187...These plates were then incubated for 24 h at 28° or 37°, and the daily generations per colony were calculated from the number of viable cells in each repr
records.notes: Calculated mu from mutation-accumulation experiment data: Table 1 gives Vf-wt mean total generations per lineage = 5187 over the stated propagation period of 217 days (single-cell bottleneck restreaking, colonies incubated 24 h/cycle at 28C on TSAN). generations/day = 5187/217 = 23.90; mu (h^-1) = ln2 x generations_per_day / 24h = 0.693x23.90/24 = 0.69 h^-1; doubling_time_h = 24/generations_per_day = 1.00 h. This represents an average growth rate over an entire colony-growth cycle on solid agar (not a liquid batch growth curve), so treated as medium confidence.

records.snip: the increase in bacterial CFUs observed in the experiment (>3-fold increase in CFUs during the first 24 h of coculture under continuous light)
records.notes: Lower-bound estimate only, because the paper reports '>3-fold' (an inequality, not an exact fold-change) over the first 24 h of coculture. mu >= ln(3)/24h = 0.046 /h; doubling time <= ln2/0.046 = 15.1 h. Growth occurs on carbon released by algal cell lysis/exudation (coculture with C. reinhardtii CC-5390), not a defined carbon substrate, so this is not a classical substrate-limited growth rate. True mu could be higher than 0.046/h since actual fold-change exceeds 3.

records.snip: After 4 h the flow of fresh medium was initiated (6 ml/h) and the chemostat culture volume was maintained at 12 ml... The chemostat populations were grown for a total of 485 generations (28 days).
records.notes: Dilution rate D = flow rate / volume = 6 mL/h / 12 mL = 0.5 /h; at chemostat steady state mu = D = 0.5/h (doubling time = ln2/0.5 = 1.39 h). Cross-checked against the paper's independently reported total generations: 485 generations / 28 days = 0.7217 generations/h -> mu = ln2 x 0.7217 = 0.500/h, consistent with the dilution-rate calculation. Sodium fumarate added as alternative electron acceptor because oxygen can be limiting in the chemostat system; base lactate mineral medium composition (Paulick et al. 2009) not detailed in this paper beyond the fumarate/gentamicin supplements.

records.notes: Growth rate = imposed dilution rate at chemostat steady state (mu = D = 0.12 h-1); doubling time = ln(2)/0.12 = 5.78 h. Glucose concentration in medium_composition is CALCULATED, not explicitly stated as a concentration: paper states stress media were generated by decreasing only the limiting element from the Balanced formulation (C:N:P=320:16:1; N=10 mM NH4Cl, P=0.625 mM K2HPO4 held constant). For C-stress ratio 40:16:1, C = (40/16) x 10 mM-N-equivalent = 25 mM C; glucose has 6 carbons, so glucose = 25/6 = 4.17 mM. Only the molar ratio (40:16:1) is explicitly given in the text; the absolute glucose concentration is a derived estimate.

records.snip: C:N:P molar ratios used for the chemostats were 320:16:1 (Balanced), 40:16:1 (C-stress) ... Cultures were grown at two different dilution rates ... 0.12 h−1 and 0.48 h−1.

Same C-stress medium as the 0.12 h-1 C-stress record (glucose concentration calculated, see that record's notes). Confidence lowered further because the authors report cell aggregation/biofilm formation at 0.48 h-1 across ALL resource treatments, casting doubt on whether cells were uniformly growing at the nominal dilution rate under this fast/stress combination.

records.notes: Text states the batch maximum specific growth rate 'exceeded 0.5 h-1' — i.e., 0.5 h-1 is reported as a lower-bound threshold, not an exact point estimate (the precise value is only shown graphically in Supplementary Fig. S1, not given numerically in the main text). Doubling time calculated from this threshold as ln(2)/0.5 = 1.39 h (a lower bound on doubling time, since actual mu could be higher). records.notes: Cells were pre-grown on methane, then harvested and resuspended as resting-cell suspensions (OD600=1) in basal LSM medium (pH 4.5) with formate (10 mM) supplied as an exogenous electron donor, chloramphenicol (1.25 mg/L) added to block growth, and no N source/trace metals provided. Rates are substrate-consumption (oxidation) rates measured over 2-3 h incubations with 1% (v/v) gas substrate in the headspace (Table in Results, resting-cell assay). These are NOT growth-coupled uptake rates (cells not growing) but are directly reported specific substrate oxidation capacities; included as uptake_rates per task scope.

records.strain: ZMB6 (engineered from ZM4; 2,3-BDO dominant-metabolism-compromised intermediate-chassis with three chromosomal copies of D-lactate dehydrogenase LmldhA from Leuconostoc mesenteroides)

records.med: Corncob residue hydrolysate (CRH) fermentation medium, scale-up fermenter
records.cite: Yan et al. Paradigm of engineering recalcitrant non-model microorganism with dominant metabolic pathway as a biorefinery chassis. Nature Communication
records.snip: In a scale-up fermenter with corncob residue hydrolysate, ZMB6 also demonstrated excellent glucose conversion capacity with a D-lactate titer of 104.6 g/L at a yield exceeding 0.97 g/g within 45 h... The titer, productiv
records.notes: Secretion rate (volumetric productivity) of 2.32 g/L/h for D-lactate is stated verbatim in the text (and is consistent with titer/time: 104.6 g/L / 45 h = 2.32 g/L/h). Uptake rate of glucose was CALCULATED as qS = qP / Yp/s = 2.32 g/L/h / 0.97 g D-lactate per g glucose = 2.39 g glucose/L/h, using the explicitly reported product yield (>0.97 g/g) and productivity. Rates are volumetric (g/L/h), not per-gDW, because no biomass (dry cell weight or OD) concentration was reported for this fermentation run, so conversion to mmol/gDW/h was not possible. Culture temperature/pH/aeration for this specific scale-up fermentation were not explicitly stated in the main text (general Z. mobilis cultivation elsewhere in Methods used 30C, but this was not tied specifically to the scale-up CRH fermentation), so left unspecified rather than assumed.

records.strain: MAG260 / isolate AN (Azonexus sp.), mixed enrichment (not axenic culture)

records.cite: Jonassen et al. Nitrous oxide respiring bacteria in biogas digestates for reduced agricultural emissions. The ISME Journal (2021). DOI:10.1038/s41396-
records.snip: The modeling indicated that the cell density of the growing NRB increased exponentially (specific growth rate, µ = 0.1 h-1) from a very low initial density (~2.5x10^3 cells mL-1) to 1.6x10^8 cells mL-1 after 110 h.
records.notes: Growth rate mu=0.1 h-1 was derived by the authors from a kinetic model of N2 production/N2O reduction fit to represent two subpopulations (one exponentially growing), and cross-validated here: ln(1.6e8/2.5e3 cells/mL)/110 h = ln(64000)/110 = 11.07/110 = 0.101 h-1, consistent with the reported 0.1 h-1 (calculation from numbers explicitly in the paper: initial density ~2.5e3 cells/mL, density 1.6e8 cells/mL at t=110h). This is a COMMUNITY-level (mixed enrichment) modeled growth rate, not a pure-culture measurement. Taxonomic attribution to MAG260/Azonexus sp. is inferential: MAG260 was one of two MAGs identified as 'growing' by metagenome/metaproteome coverage regression and accounted for 92% of the total detectable Nos (N2O reductase) protein pool at the final timepoint, and isolate AN (Azonexus sp.) shared 98.2% ANI with MAG260, but the mu=0.1 h-1 value itself was derived from bulk N2O/N2 gas kinetics of the whole enrichment, not an isolate-specific measurement. Confidence marked low due to lack of species-pure-culture resolution. Enrichment incubation temperature (20C) explicitly stated in text; incubated at 20C rather than digester temperatures (37/52C) to avoid selecting for organisms outside the normal soil temperature range.

records.org: ANME-2c archaeon in syntrophic consortium with sulfate-reducing bacteria (marine anaerobic methanotrophic archaea)

records.med: Synthetic marine sulfate-reducer medium, pH 7.4, methane headspace
records.cite: Müller et al. Atomic resolution structures of the methane-activating enzyme in anaerobic methanotrophy reveal extensive post-translational modificatio
records.snip: sediment slurries were set up with a synthetic marine sulfate reducer medium at pH 7.4 and methane headspace at 20 °C... The activity-based doubling time for both cultures, monitored by sulfide measurements, was about 4
records.notes: Paper directly reports (Methods, 'ANME-2c cultivation') an activity-based doubling time of 'about 4 to 6 months' for methane-oxidizing/sulfate-reducing enrichment cultures grown on methane (electron donor) + sulfate (electron acceptor) in synthetic marine sulfate-reducer medium at pH 7.4, 20 degC, monitored via sulfide production. Doubling_time_h and growth_rate_per_h fields here use the midpoint of the reported range (5 months = 3600 h): mu = ln2/3600 = 0.0001925 h^-1. Range bounds: 4 months (2880 h) -> mu=0.000241 h^-1; 6 months (4320 h) -> mu=0.0001605 h^-1. This is a mixed archaeal-bacterial syntrophic consortium, not an axenic culture; rate reflects population-level (not single-species) activity.

records.snip: we estimated that the experimentally observed X. fastidiosa growth rate (0.1608 day-1) (ref 19) was recovered in silico... This reduction converted the generation time from 1.45 h (typical optimal bacterial growth) to 10
records.notes: The paper explicitly states an experimentally observed X. fastidiosa growth rate of 0.1608 day^-1 (attributed to Leite et al. 2004, ref 19, a xylem-chemistry-based-medium study; original culture conditions such as strain/temperature/pH not restated in this paper). growth_rate_per_h computed by unit conversion: 0.1608 day-1 / 24 = 0.0067 h-1. doubling_time_h = 103 h is directly reported in the same paragraph ('converted the generation time... to 103 h (fastidious growth)'); cross-check: ln2/0.0067 = 103.4 h, consistent with the paper's stated 103 h. Abstract also states generation time can range 10-100 h for some strains (not organism/medium-specific enough to record separately).

records.med: in vivo growth within C. elegans intestine (worm pre-fed pathogen lawn grown 24h at 30C in LB, then plated on SK agar)
records.cite: Biancalani et al. Disentangling bacterial invasiveness from lethality in an experimental host‐pathogen system. Molecular Systems Biology (2019). DOI:1
records.snip: we determined the colonization rates c and the growth rates r by inspection. We found similar values for the growth rates of the three species (r ~ 0.08/h)
records.notes: Not a conventional defined-medium growth-rate measurement: r is a population-dynamics model parameter (pathogen growth rate inside the C. elegans gut, eq. dN/dt=(rN+c)(1-N/K)) fitted 'by inspection' to pathogen-load time-course data (Fig 3B), not measured directly from an OD/CFU slope in vitro. Value explicitly stated in text as an order-of-magnitude estimate shared across all three pathogen species (P. aeruginosa, S. marcescens, S. enterica). No defined medium composition, temperature, or pH is given for the in-host growth itself (host maintained at 25C per Materials and Methods; pathogen lawns were grown from monocultures for 24h at 30C in 5 mL LB to saturation before exposure, but that pre-culture step is not what r describes).

records.snip: After 6 h, the number of viable Ef cells increased approximately 50-fold, indicating favorable growth conditions. This corresponds to a doubling time of ~1 h, which is slower than in the brain-heart infusion (BHI) medium
records.notes: Doubling time ~1 h is stated directly in text (derived by the authors from a ~50-fold CFU increase over 6 h: log2(50)=~5.6 doublings in 6 h -> ~1.07 h/doubling, rounds to their reported ~1 h). growth_rate_per_h = ln2/1 = 0.693/h (calculated by curator from the reported doubling time). Independently corroborated later in Methods: 'The infection progressed for 6 h, which resulted in ~6 generations' for the Tn-seq library in the same mucosal Transwell system (6 generations/6 h = 1 h/doubling), consistent with the CFU-based estimate.

records.cite: Mikhaleva et al. Gut microbiota-derived metabolites tune host homeostasis fate. mSystems (2026). DOI:10.1128/msystems.01304-25. [PMC13098192]
records.snip: the inoculum used during the infections was grown in liquids for the same amount of duplication as the infections...the inoculum was diluted in BHI and Basal medium (3 mL) to an OD600 of 0.02 for 3.5 h and 5 h, respectiv
records.notes: Calculated: the BHI control culture was grown for 3.5 h to match the same number of doublings (~6 generations, stated elsewhere in Methods for the matched mucosal infection) reached by the library in the mucosal Transwell over 6 h. doubling_time = 3.5 h / 6 generations = 0.583 h; growth_rate_per_h = ln2/0.583 = 1.19/h. This is an indirect calculation combining two statements in the Methods (the '3.5 h' BHI control duration and the '~6 generations' figure given for the matched infection), so confidence is medium rather than high.

records.snip: Hydraulic/solids retention time (days): 30; pH: 8.0 ± 0.3... Influent acetate concentration (mM): 75... Effluent acetate (mM): 2.0 ± 0.4. Values represent an average...during the period of steady-state performance (days
records.notes: Continuous stirred-tank chemostat (5 L reactors), 55°C, fed 160 mL/day into 3.2 L working volume (SRT=HRT increased to 30 days after day 126; steady-state Table 1 values are averaged over days 200-300, i.e. the 30-day-HRT period). growth_rate_per_h = dilution rate D = 1/HRT = 1/(30 d x 24 h/d) = 0.001389 /h, assuming chemostat steady state (mu=D). doubling_time_h = ln2/mu = 499.1 h. Volumetric acetate uptake rate calculated as qS = D x (S_influent - S_effluent) = 0.001389 /h x (75-2.0) mM = 0.1014 mmol/L/h; this is a community bulk/volumetric rate, NOT normalized to biomass (gDW), because no biomass/VSS concentration was reported in the main text (only Total Solids 8.5 g/L, which includes non-biomass solids).

records.notes: Batch microcosms (40 mL serum bottles, 55°C, 100 rpm, N2:CO2 headspace) inoculated with digestate from the 300-day chemostat enrichment; acetate amended at 50 mM (3000 mg/L). No biomass (gDW/L) was reported for these microcosms in the main text, so this is a bulk volumetric rate, not a per-gDW specific rate; no growth rate could be calculated (no biomass or cell-count time course reported).

records.snip: After a 10% inoculation, the cell recycle membrane bioreactor was operated in batch mode for 9 h before switching to continuous mode with total cell recycle... When an OD600nm of 200 was reached, a cell bleeding rate was
records.notes: Membrane cell-recycle bioreactor: biomass is retained by the membrane and only removed via a bleed stream; at steady state specific growth rate mu equals the bleed dilution rate (mass-balance identity for perfect cell-recycle systems), which the paper explicitly states was set to 0.05 h-1. growth_rate_per_h calculated as mu = D_bleed = 0.05 h-1 (doubling_time_h = ln2/0.05 = 13.86 h); this is an inference from the recycle-reactor mass balance, not a directly reported specific growth rate, hence medium confidence. Productivity 15.5 g/L/h and titre 15.4 g/L and yield 0.31 g/g are as reported (volumetric, not per-gDW).

records.med: Minimal medium, glucose:benzoate (1:1) carbon mixture, Fe-replete
records.cite: Mendonca et al. Hierarchical routing in carbon metabolism favors iron-scavenging strategy in iron-deficient soil Pseudomonas species. Proceedings of t
records.snip: there were near-equal consumption rates of both substrates in the Fe-replete cells (19.8 ± 4.4 mmol C gCDW-1 h-1 and 27.2 ± 8.1 mmol C gCDW-1 h-1 for glucose and benzoate, respectively)
records.notes: Directly reported specific consumption (uptake) rates for P. putida KT2440 grown on an equimolar glucose:benzoate carbon mixture (100 mM C total) under Fe-replete condition (30 µM unchelated Fe). Sum of the two uptake rates (19.8+27.2=47.0 mmol C/gCDW/h) matches the separately reported 'carbon influx rate' of 47.0 ± 9.2 mM C gCDW-1 h-1 for Fe-replete cells, confirming internal consistency; not entered as a separate record to avoid double counting. CO2 efflux (19.6 ± 0.98 mM C gCDW-1 h-1) reported as an estimated/calculated flux from central-carbon-metabolism flux balancing in the same Fe-replete condition. Culture temperature/pH not stated in main text (detailed in SI Appendix, not available in this XML); oxygen state inferred as aerobic given this is an obligate aerobic soil bacterium with measured TCA-cycle/CO2 efflux.

records.med: Minimal medium, glucose:benzoate (1:1) carbon mixture, severe Fe-limited, phase 1 (benzoate-consumption phase)
records.cite: Mendonca et al. Hierarchical routing in carbon metabolism favors iron-scavenging strategy in iron-deficient soil Pseudomonas species. Proceedings of t
records.snip: for the severe Fe-limited condition, there were two clear phases of substrate depletion wherein benzoate depletion (64.5 ± 1.2 mmol C gCDW-1 h-1) occurred rapidly prior to the start of the glucose depletion
records.notes: Directly reported rates for P. putida KT2440 under severe Fe limitation (38 nM unchelated Fe) during phase 1 (benzoate is primary substrate consumed from the glucose:benzoate mixture). PVD secretion rate 91.8 ± 15.2 µmol gCDW-1 h-1 and gluconate secretion 11.6 ± 4.6 µmol gCDW-1 h-1 explicitly reported for this same phase. A separately reported carbon-influx estimate for this phase (66.1 ± 1.4 mM C gCDW-1 h-1, '41% higher' than Fe-replete 47.0) closely matches the benzoate uptake rate and was not entered separately to avoid redundancy.

records.med: Minimal medium, glucose:benzoate (1:1) carbon mixture, severe Fe-limited, phase 2 (glucose-consumption phase)
records.cite: Mendonca et al. Hierarchical routing in carbon metabolism favors iron-scavenging strategy in iron-deficient soil Pseudomonas species. Proceedings of t
records.snip: benzoate depletion ... occurred rapidly prior to the start of the glucose depletion (15.2 ± 0.2 mmol C gCDW-1 h-1) ... gluconate secretion (from 11.6 ± 4.6 µmol gCDW-1 h-1 to 2.3 ± 0.3 mmol gCDW-1 h-1) during the glucose
records.notes: Directly reported rates for P. putida KT2440 under severe Fe limitation (38 nM unchelated Fe) during phase 2 (glucose is the remaining substrate after benzoate depletion). Glucose uptake 15.2 ± 0.2 mmol C gCDW-1 h-1; PVD secretion 17.5 ± 2.86 µmol gCDW-1 h-1; gluconate secretion increased ~200-fold to 2.3 ± 0.3 mmol gCDW-1 h-1 vs phase 1. Total carbon efflux (26.7 ± 2.7 mM C gCDW-1 h-1) explicitly reported and noted by authors as showing 'large discrepancy' vs the carbon influx of 15.2 ± 0.2 mM C gCDW-1 h-1 (i.e., matches glucose uptake number), reflecting release of previously stored/overflow carbon (e.g., gluconate + CO2).

records.cite: Szul et al. Carbon Fate and Flux in Prochlorococcus under Nitrogen Limitation. mSystems (2019). DOI:10.1128/mSystems.00254-18. [PMC6392094]
records.snip: the dilution rate of the chemostat (which in steady state is the growth rate of the population) was well below the rate for one cell doubling per day (0.09 versus 0.69 day−1)
records.notes: Paper reports N-limited chemostat dilution rate D = 0.09 day^-1 and states that at chemostat steady state this equals the population growth rate (mu). Converted units: mu (h^-1) = 0.09 day^-1 / 24 h = 0.00375 h^-1. Doubling time = ln(2)/mu = 0.6931/0.00375 = 184.8 h. Medium base is modified N-limited AMP-A (5 mM TAPS buffer, 16 uM NH4Cl, prepared in N-free Turks Island Salt Mix basal salts; exact salt concentrations not given in text). Culture grown at 22C under 14h graduated light/10h dark cycle mimicking natural daylight (light levels not quantified numerically in extractable text). Oxygen status not explicitly stated (Prochlorococcus is an oxygenic photoautotroph, so aerobic is a reasonable but not explicitly confirmed inference; left null per strict-accuracy rule). No N-replete (batch) numeric growth rate was reported/calculable from explicit numbers in the text (only qualitative descriptions of log-phase growth after NH4+ rescue).

records.cite: Yang et al. Staphylococcus aureus alpha-toxin impairs early neutrophil localization via electrogenic disruption of store-operated calcium entry. Cell
records.snip: S. aureus has a rapid doubling time in rich media of 20 min.
records.notes: This is a generic background statement in the Discussion (not a primary experimental measurement performed in this immunology-focused study), with no strain, exact medium composition, or culture conditions (temp/pH) specified beyond 'rich media'. Doubling time (20 min = 0.333 h) is stated directly in the paper (growth_method=reported); growth_rate_per_h = ln(2)/0.333 h = 2.08 h^-1 was calculated from this stated doubling time. Flagged low confidence because it is not tied to a specific defined medium/strain/experiment within this paper.

records.mode: chemostat (continuous-flow bioreactor operated at fixed hydraulic/solids retention time)
records.med: Continuous bioreactor fed lignocellulosic biorefinery conversion residue (complex organic substrate)
records.cite: Scarborough et al. Diagnosing and Predicting Mixed-Culture Fermentations with Unicellular and Guild-Based Metabolic Models. mSystems (2020). DOI:10.11
records.snip: We constrained iFermCell215 by setting the growth rate of the model to 0.0069 h−1, corresponding to the bioreactor retention time of 6 days...The bioreactor performance data are from day 96 of reactor operations describe
records.notes: mu = 1/retention time (chemostat steady state). Paper explicitly states retention time = 6 days and states this corresponds to growth rate 0.0069 h-1 (we verified: 1/(6 d x 24 h/d) = 0.00694 h-1, matches paper's reported value). Reactor is a real self-assembled mixed microbial community (not a single named prokaryote species) fed lignocellulosic biorefinery residue for 96 days; data originate from a previously published bioreactor (Scarborough et al. 2018) and are used here as a constraint for a guild-based genome-scale model. Temperature (35 C) and neutral pH are generic model assumptions stated for the thermodynamic calculations in Methods, not confirmed as the exact bioreactor operating temperature/pH; flagged medium confidence for that reason. A second growth rate (0.0051 h-1, corresponding to a model-predicted 8-day retention time) appears in the same paragraph as a MODEL PREDICTION (not an independent experimental observation) and was not recorded as a separate entry.

records.cite: Thevasundaram et al. Engineering nonphotosynthetic carbon fixation for production of bioplastics by methanogenic archaea. Proceedings of the National
records.snip: ...its fully sequenced, relatively simple genome; growth on plates as single colonies; relatively fast doubling time for methanogens (∼2 h); and published genetic tools and expression vectors...
records.notes: Doubling time (~2 h) is stated explicitly in the paper as part of the rationale for choosing M. maripaludis as a chassis, citing prior literature (refs 25-26) rather than a new measurement performed in this study; not tied to a single specific medium/condition in the sentence itself. Optimized batch growth conditions reported elsewhere in Methods (30 C, 250 rpm, H2/CO2 80/20 to 275 kPa every 24 h, 14 d) were used for temperature/oxygen/culture_mode fields but are not necessarily the exact condition under which the ~2 h doubling time was originally measured. Product titers (S-3HB up to 16.3 +/- 2.7 mg/L in McNA-Ala at 7 d; PHB up to 171 +/- 4 mg/L, 24.0 +/- 1.9% of dry cell weight at 14 d) are reported but could not be converted to a specific secretion rate (mmol/gDW/h) because absolute biomass concentration (g DCW/L) is not given in the extracted text, only %DCW content.

records.med: Bicarbonate-buffered freshwater medium with naphthalene (dissolved in 2,2,4,4,6,8,8-heptamethylnonane) as carbon/energy source and sulfate as electron acceptor
records.cite: Dong et al. Fermentative Spirochaetes mediate necromass recycling in anoxic hydrocarbon-contaminated habitats. The ISME Journal (2018). DOI:10.1038/s4
records.snip: if the culture grows exponentially from a 10% parent inoculum to full density (i.e., the cell numbers increased tenfold) in 6 weeks, the doubling time is approximately 13 days.
records.notes: Doubling time calculated by the paper's authors from an assumed 10-fold increase in cell numbers over 6 weeks (42 days), i.e. exponential growth n(t)/n(0)=2^(t/td) => td = t*ln2/ln(10) = 42 d * 0.693/2.303 = 12.6 d ~ 13 d (paper's own stated approximation, which we reproduce). This is a speculative back-of-envelope estimate based on a typical assumed anaerobic growth yield (5-10% of consumed substrate), not a directly measured OD/cell-count time course; hence low confidence. Converted to h: 13 d x 24 h/d = 312 h; mu = ln2/312 h = 0.00222 h-1. Temperature/pH not explicitly stated for the co-culture enrichment N47 itself in the extracted text (30 C and pH 7.0 were reported only for the separately isolated pure culture of R. cohabitans, not confirmed identical for the N47 co-culture), so left null to avoid inventing.

Converted: 11 d x 24 h/d = 264 h; mu = ln2/264 h = 0.00263-0.00266 h-1. This is a speculative, doubly-derived estimate (assumed growth yield + assumed inoculum fraction), so confidence is low; recorded as a distinct observation from the naive (uncorrected) 13-day estimate because the paper presents both explicitly with different input numbers.

records.cite: Held et al. Nutrient colimitation is a quantitative, dynamic property of microbial populations. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the
records.snip: Three replicate measurements of growth rate...for E. coli in minimal medium...The blue surface is a model fit to all replicate measurements (Poisson arrival time model with g_max ~ 0.66 per hour, a_glu ~ 23 per hour per
records.notes: g_max (0.66 h^-1) is the fitted asymptotic maximum specific growth rate parameter from a Poisson-arrival-time (colimitation) model nonlinearly least-squares fit (scipy.optimize.curve_fit) to three replicate OD600 growth-curve-derived growth rate measurements of E. coli K-12 MG1655 across a full-factorial glucose (0-40 mM) x ammonium chloride (0-40 mM) concentration gradient in modified M9 minimal medium (96-well plate reader, 37C, 500 RPM shaking, 24 h). Value is stated explicitly in the paper (Fig. 2 legend / Materials and Methods) as the model's g_max parameter, i.e., the extrapolated growth rate at saturating glucose and ammonium, not a single raw measured point. No biomass-yield (gDW) or substrate-consumption data were reported in units convertible to mmol/gDW/h, so no uptake_rates could be computed; the paper's co-reported affinity parameters a_glu (~23 h^-1 mM^-1) and a_amm (~90 h^-1 mM^-1) are Monod-type initial-slope kinetic coefficients (units per hour per mM), not substrate uptake rates, and were not recorded as uptake_rates to avoid unit mismatch.

records.cite: Schmitz et al. The thermoacidophilic methanotroph Methylacidiphilum fumariolicum SolV oxidizes subatmospheric H2 with a high-affinity, membrane-associ
records.snip: The maximum H2 oxidation rate (Vmax(app)) was measured to be 340 nmol H2 min−1 mg DW−1 and the cells have an apparent high affinity (Km(app)) for H2 of 195 ± 10 nM (n = 8).
records.notes: Whole-cell H2 uptake Vmax(app) reported as 340 nmol H2 min-1 mgDW-1 (n=8, measured by membrane-inlet mass spectrometry, MIMS, on cells cultivated in a 7L bioreactor/chemostat at 55C, pH 3.0, DO 0.2-1% air saturation, fed CH4/CO2/H2 gas mixture; cells pre-grown such that only the high-affinity Hyd-1h hydrogenase was expressed). Converted here: 340 nmol/min/mgDW x 60 min/h = 20,400 nmol/h/mgDW = 20.4 µmol/h/mgDW = 20.4 mmol/h/gDW (unit identity: 1 µmol/mg = 1 mmol/g). This is an apparent Vmax under H2-saturating kinetic assay conditions (Michaelis-Menten fit), not necessarily the actual in-situ growth-associated flux; Km(app)=195±10nM for H2 (affinity, not itself an uptake rate, reported for context only, not entered as a separate uptake_rates row). No growth rate or doubling time is reported anywhere in this paper for M. fumariolicum SolV.

records.notes: Generation (doubling) time of 1.6 h is directly reported for evolved strain C11 under HL(983 µmol photons m-2 s-1)/HT(41 °C)/HC(3% CO2), grown in BG11 + 10 mM NaHCO3 in a MC 1000-OD Multicultivator; growth_rate_per_h calculated as mu = ln2/1.6 = 0.4332 h-1. Paper's own Methods define doubling time = Ln2/K' where K' is slope of Ln(OD720) in early exponential phase, so this is a reported/paper-calculated value we further convert to mu using the same standard formula. Text also states this represents a '654% increase compared to the wild-type (wt)' but the wt's absolute doubling time under this exact HL/HT/HC combination is not given as a number in the main text (only shown graphically in Fig. 1B), so no wt record is created to avoid inventing a value. Oxygen marked aerobic as photoautotrophic cyanobacterial culture (not explicitly labeled in text). pH not stated.
