records.cite: Meynard-Doumenc et al. Oxidative stress constrains evolution of bacteriophage host-range diversity. The ISME Journal (2026). DOI:10.1093/ismejo/wrag09
records.snip: A continuous culture was set up with a constant flow of 70 ml/h of LB medium added and removed from the fermenter using two peristaltic pumps. ... Two millilitres of culture (OD600nm = 0.8) of each bacterial strain were
records.notes: Chemostat dilution rate D = inflow rate / working volume = 70 ml/h / 200 ml = 0.35 h-1, explicitly stated flow (70 ml/h) and vessel volume (200 ml) in Methods. At chemostat steady state, specific growth rate mu = D = 0.35 h-1 (standard chemostat theory). Doubling time = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.35 = 1.98 h. This is a mixed 3-strain community chemostat (MG1655, LF82, MEc1) plus a lytic phage (LF82_P10); the dilution rate constrains the community-level steady-state growth rate, not necessarily each strain's intrinsic mu individually since strains differ in phage susceptibility and may not each be independently at D. Separate batch-culture growth-rate comparisons (with/without H2O2) were performed via OD600 log-slope but no numeric mu values are given in the body text (only in a supplementary figure, S1B), so those are not extracted here.

records.snip: the biofilm grew in a constant zone of 100 μm at a constant rate of 7 μm/hour (Fig. 3, D and E)... Δr/Δt = d(v - d^2/2r); under r≫d, Δr/Δt ≈ dv
records.notes: Calculated using paper's own explicit biofilm-growth model: growth-front expansion rate Δr/Δt=7 µm/h, growth-zone width d=100 µm (both explicitly reported, glycerol M63B1 condition, before H2O2 addition). Paper states for r≫d (valid here since biofilm radius always exceeded 300 µm >> d=100 µm), Δr/Δt ≈ d·v, so v = (Δr/Δt)/d = 7/100 = 0.07 h^-1 (v defined by the paper as the intrinsic bacterial specific growth rate = ln2/doubling time). td = ln2/0.07 = 9.9h. Confidence medium because this requires applying the paper's r≫d approximation rather than a directly stated mu value.

records.snip: The values of rE, rS, and rC are based on empirically determined exponential growth rates for E. coli and S. Typhimurium in LB, which are approximately 0.033 min-1 for both these species. For a generation time of approxi
records.notes: Paper states empirically determined exponential growth rate of 0.033 min-1 for E. coli in LB (used to parameterize a Lotka-Volterra invasion model); converted to per-hour: mu = 0.033 min-1 * 60 = 1.98 h-1. Doubling time = ln2/mu = 0.693/1.98 = 0.35 h (21 min), consistent with the paper's stated ~20 min generation time. This value is attributed to cited prior work (refs 39,40) rather than a new measurement in this paper, and no strain designation or medium composition beyond 'LB' is given.

records.snip: The main substrate uptake rates were set as the maximum uptake rates for each growth substrate...16 mmol methanol gDW-1 h-1 and 8 mmol acetate gDW-1 h-1 (Elferink et al, 1994)...
records.notes: Maximal specific methanol uptake rate (16 mmol gDW-1 h-1) for M. barkeri Fusaro is stated directly in the text, derived by the authors (Feist et al.) from biomass yields and maximal specific growth rates reported in Elferink et al. 1994 (a cited primary source, not a new measurement in this paper). Growth yield on methanol reported elsewhere in the text as 5-7 gDW per mol CH4 (Smith and Mah 1978; Weimer and Zeikus 1978; Hippe et al 1979), an organism-level methane yield, not a growth rate; not converted here for lack of a substrate:CH4 stoichiometric factor stated in the text. M. barkeri is an archaeon (methanogen), included as a prokaryote per broad two-empire usage.

records.notes: This is a field (in situ) isotope-uptake measurement (NanoSIMS, 13C-bicarbonate and 15N-ammonium) of a natural Prochlorococcus population at the deep chlorophyll maximum, not a defined-medium lab culture, so culture_mode/medium fields do not map onto standard batch/chemostat categories. growth_rate_per_h computed from the paper-stated doubling time of ~6 days: mu = ln2/(6*24h) = 0.693/144 = 0.00667 h-1 (approximately consistent with, but not identical to, the directly reported µN of 0.16 day-1 = 0.00667 h-1 -- these two numbers as given by the authors are self-consistent). µC (0.024 day-1) is reported separately and was stated to be too low to support the growth rates implied by cell-cycle-based estimates (0.14-0.25 day-1, cited from refs 27-30, not this paper's own measurement, so not separately recorded as a growth_rate here).

records.cite: Shahreen et al. Enzyme-constrained metabolic model of Treponema pallidum identified glycerol-3-phosphate dehydrogenase as an alternate electron sink.
records.snip: In 1981, Fieldsteel et al. reported the short-term multiplication of T. pallidum in a coculture system with rabbit epithelial cells, achieving up to 100-fold growth over 12 to 18 days
records.notes: This paper (introduction) cites Fieldsteel et al. 1981 (ref B6) reporting ~100-fold multiplication of T. pallidum over 12-18 days in a rabbit-epithelial-cell coculture system; medium/temperature/pH not detailed in this citing text. Assuming exponential growth, mu = ln(100)/t. For t=12 d (288 h): mu=4.6052/288=0.01599 h-1 (doubling time 43.3 h); for t=18 d (432 h): mu=4.6052/432=0.01066 h-1 (doubling time 65.0 h). Reported growth_rate_per_h/doubling_time_h here use the slower (18-day, more conservative) bound (~0.011 h-1, ~63 h doubling time); true value lies between 0.0107 and 0.0160 h-1 depending on which end of the 12-18 day range applies. Low confidence: numbers originate from a paper (Fieldsteel 1981) not itself in this extraction batch, only summarized/cited here; no medium composition given in the citing text.

records.cite: Yu et al. Bacterial chemolithoautotrophy via manganese oxidation. Nature (2020). DOI:10.1038/s41586-020-2468-5. [PMC7802741]
records.snip: Concurrent with Mn(II) oxidation, Species A exhibited sustained exponential growth, roughly matched by the less numerous and perhaps commensal Species B (Td = 6.1 and 7.7 day, respectively). The co-culture oxidised Mn(II
records.notes: Paper reports doubling time Td=6.1 day for Species A during exponential Mn(II)-dependent growth (Fig. 2b; Extended Data Fig. 2b). Converted: doubling_time_h = 6.1 day x 24 h/day = 146.4 h. growth_rate_per_h = ln(2)/146.4 = 0.00473 h-1 (formula mu=ln2/Td). The Mn(II) oxidation rate (qMn, 3.4-9.0e-15 mol Mn(II) cell-1 h-1) is explicitly reported in the text but represents the COMBINED co-culture (Species A + Species B together), since the two species could not be separately quantified for this rate; included for context, not attributed solely to Species A. A separate, distinct metric (rate of Mn(II)-oxidation-rate doubling, not biomass doubling) was also reported: initially doubling every 6.2 days (s.d.=1.1, n=4 replicate cultures) before decelerating to 10.8 days (s.d.=0.1); this describes acceleration/deceleration of the oxidation reaction rate itself, not restated here as growth_rate. Overall culture pH not explicitly stated (only component buffer pH 7.2 for phosphate stock is given).

records.cite: Sayin et al. Bacterial population dynamics during colonization of solid tumors. Molecular Systems Biology (2025). DOI:10.1038/s44320-025-00175-5. [PMC
records.snip: If the most successful clone reaches a population size of 2.5 × 10^8 within a day of injection, it divides roughly 28 times during that period (corresponding to a generation time of ~50 min).
records.notes: Calculated in-vivo growth estimate for the leading (largest) bacterial clone colonizing a tumor after intravenous injection. Inputs from paper: final population N=2.5×10^8 cells, starting from a single founder cell, elapsed time t=1 day=24 h, giving doublings=log2(2.5e8)=~27.9 (paper states 'roughly 28'), generation time = 24h/28 = 0.857 h (paper rounds to '~50 min' = 0.833 h, used here). growth_rate_per_h = ln2/0.833 = 0.832 h-1 (calculated). This is an in vivo estimate, not a defined-medium culture; temperature taken as normal mouse body temperature (37°C), not explicitly restated in the quoted sentence. Oxygen tension inside solid tumors is typically reduced/heterogeneous but not quantified in the paper.

records.strain: pan-genome collection Tn-seq libraries (21 strains, e.g. TIGR4, D39)
records.cite: Rosconi et al. A bacterial pan-genome makes gene essentiality strain-dependent and evolvable. Nature Microbiology (2022). DOI:10.1038/s41564-022-01208
records.snip: Tn-seq libraries were first grown in THY until an OD600 of ~0.5, pelleted and diluted to an OD600 of 0.1 in SDMM, with 20 mM glucose as carbon source. Cells were grown for 4 generations (~3 h).
records.notes: Calculated growth rate from stated number of generations and elapsed time for Tn-seq fitness assay in SDMM: doubling_time_h = 3 h / 4 generations = 0.75 h; growth_rate_per_h = ln(2)/0.75 = 0.924 h^-1. Both '4 generations' and '~3 h' are stated as approximate in the Methods. Applies generically to the Tn-seq library growth step used across the 21-strain pan-genome collection (protocol not strain-specific); temperature (37 C, 5% CO2) and pH (7.3) taken from the general bacterial strains/culture-conditions Methods paragraph describing SDMM liquid cultures with oxyrase, not restated in the immediate sentence.

records.notes: Doubling time (~20 min = 0.333 h) explicitly reported in text (a review statement citing refs 5,12-15, not primary data generated in this paper). growth_rate_per_h calculated as ln(2)/0.333 = 2.079 h^-1. No strain identity, medium composition, temperature, or pH is given in this review, so those fields are left null/unspecified; confidence downgraded accordingly.

No medium, temperature, pH, or strain identity given in this review; confidence downgraded accordingly.

records.cite: Lee et al. Bacterial charity work leads to population-wide resistance. Nature (2010). DOI:10.1038/nature09354. [PMC2936489]
records.snip: In the bioreactor, E. coli were continuously cultured with a dilution rate of 0.48h-1. ... Bacterial cultures were grown at 37°C in Luria-Bertani (LB) media obtained from Fisher Scientific.
records.notes: Dilution rate D = 0.48 h-1 is explicitly reported for the continuous-culture (chemostat) bioreactor used to evolve norfloxacin resistance in wildtype E. coli MG1655. growth_rate_per_h = D (chemostat steady-state assumption: mu = D). doubling_time_h = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.48 = 1.44 h (calculated). Medium (LB) and temperature (37°C) stated in Methods ('Bacterial strains, media, and chemicals'); oxygenation status and pH not explicitly stated in the paper so left blank. This is the pre-selection baseline chemostat operating condition (before/at initiation of norfloxacin dosing), not an antibiotic-stressed measurement.

records.cite: Liu et al. Minimization of proteome reallocation explains metabolic transition in hierarchical utilization of carbon sources. mSystems (2025). DOI:10.
records.snip: In the glucose utilization phase (Pgluc, 0-12 h), glucose was exclusively consumed, and lactate was rapidly produced, with the lactate yield on glucose being 0.897 g lactate/g of glucose...
records.notes: Batch fermentation of B. coagulans DSM1=ATCC7050 on mixed glucose(20 g/L)+trehalose(20 g/L) in a 5 L bioreactor (4 L working volume), 50°C, 100 rpm, 7.2 L/h aeration, pH auto-controlled at 5.5 (Ca(OH)2). During the glucose-utilization phase (Pgluc, 0-12 h) lactate yield on glucose = 0.897 g/g, directly reported (not per-time specific rate; no explicit growth rate/mu or doubling time given in the paper for this phase, so growth_rate_per_h left null). Units are mass yield (g product/g substrate), not mmol/gDW/h, because no biomass/time data needed for that conversion were given explicitly.

records.cite: Liu et al. Minimization of proteome reallocation explains metabolic transition in hierarchical utilization of carbon sources. mSystems (2025). DOI:10.
records.snip: In the trehalose utilization phase (Ptre, 16-84 h), trehalose was consumed, and lactate was produced again... the lactate yield on trehalose in Ptre was only 0.526 g lactate/g of trehalose...
records.notes: Same batch fermentation as the Pgluc record above, later trehalose-utilization phase (Ptre, 16-84 h) after a lag phase (Plag, 12-16 h) triggered by glucose depletion. records.notes: Calculated mu = ln(OD_end/OD_start)/time using OD600 0.4 -> ~4 (average of Experiment 1.06 OD=4 and Experiment 1.10 OD=3.68, mean OD=3.84) over the stated 3 h interval: mu = ln(3.84/0.4)/3 = 0.755 /h; doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.92 h. CAVEAT: the paper itself describes the endpoint of this interval as reaching 'stationary' phase, meaning growth decelerated during the 3 h window (culture transitions from exponential to stationary), so this calculated mu is a lower-bound/average over a non-purely-exponential interval and likely underestimates the true mid-exponential specific growth rate. Grown at 37C with shaking (300 rpm, aerobic) per Methods; antibiotics (kanamycin 50ug/mL or carbenicillin 100ug/mL) present per plasmid selection, exact concentration used for this specific strain/experiment not disambiguated in text.

Specific formate uptake rate calculated as qS = mu / Yxs using the biomass yield reported in the same paragraph for K4Me2 (3.3 ± 0.1 gDCW/mol formate = 0.0033 gDCW/mmol formate): qS = 0.1575 / 0.0033 = 47.7 mmol formate gDCW-1 h-1. records.snip: ...medium was continuously supplied at a dilution rate of 0.26 per day... The chemostat was run without external interruption for over 3 months, approximately 37 microbial generations at the dilution rate applied (0.26 p
records.notes: growth_rate_per_h = dilution rate D, using standard chemostat steady-state theory (mu = D for any population that persists without washout, over ~37 generations of stable operation): reported D = 0.26 per day, converted to per hour: 0.26/24 = 0.010833 h-1. doubling_time_h = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.010833 = 63.98 h. This is a COMMUNITY-level rate applying jointly to the 5 coexisting dominant clades (not a single axenic species measurement); organism field lists all identified dominant taxa. Oxygen uptake rate (20 mmol/g protein/h) is reported directly in the paper (not gDW-normalized; units are per gram of cell protein) and represents an upper-bound estimate for the mixed community, not species-resolved.

records.notes: Doubling time and growth rate calculated, not directly reported, from the serial-transfer protocol: paper states a fixed daily 1:256 dilution yields 'eight generations of growth' in 24 h (2^8=256). doubling_time_h = 24 h / 8 generations = 3.0 h; growth_rate_per_h (mu) = ln(2)/3.0 = 0.231 /h. records.snip: Cultures, except the considerably slower C5 culture, doubled within 13-40 d (Supplementary Table 1). Activity doubling times were determined from the development of sulfide concentrations during the first two dilutions.
records.notes: Paper explicitly reports 'activity doubling times' of 13-40 d (excluding the slower C5-alkane culture), derived from an exponential fit (y=n*e^mx) to H2S accumulation during the first two dilution cycles of anaerobic alkane-oxidizing, sulfate-reducing enrichment cultures at 70C. Converted range: 13 d=312 h -> mu=ln2/312=0.00222 /h; 40 d=960 h -> mu=ln2/960=0.00072 /h. Reported here as a range (doubling_time_h=312-960 h, growth_rate_per_h=0.0007-0.0022 /h; single representative slow-end values placed in the numeric fields, full range documented here). This is an 'activity doubling time' (sulfide-production proxy) for a syntrophic archaea+bacteria consortium, not an axenic pure-culture growth rate; no per-gDW substrate uptake or H2S secretion rate could be computed because no biomass (dry weight) data are given.

records.snip: Fit of chemostat theory to rpoS evolution trajectories in pH 7 glucose-limited chemostats at three different dilution rates... temperature stress response of the community from one chemostat... delta=0.3 h-1 chemostats a
records.notes: Growth rate = dilution rate at chemostat steady state (mu = D), a standard chemostat relation; the paper explicitly reports one of the tested dilution rates as delta=0.3 h-1 (pH 7, glucose-limited chemostat) while reanalyzing the classical Notley-McRobb et al. E. coli rpoS chemostat-evolution dataset (ref. 11) to validate a growth-debt model. Two other dilution rates were tested ('three different dilution rates') but their numeric values are not given in the main text (only visible in the source figure), so only the one explicit value (0.3 h-1) is recorded. Exact strain designations, temperature, and full medium composition are not stated in this paper (data borrowed from an external classical experiment); confidence set to medium accordingly.

records.cite: Read et al. Nitrogen cost minimization is promoted by structural changes in the transcriptome of N-deprived Prochlorococcus cells. The ISME Journal (2
records.snip: For these batch source cultures, fluorescence-based measurements yielded a growth rate of 0.60 d-1 +/-0.01 (1 standard deviation)
records.notes: Growth rate reported directly in paper as 0.60 d-1 +/- 0.01 (fluorescence-based, log-linear portion of bulk culture fluorescence growth curve). records.cite: Khazaei et al. Metabolic multistability and hysteresis in a model aerobe-anaerobe microbiome community. Science Advances (2020). DOI:10.1126/sciadv.ab
records.snip: a residence time of 5 hours (input and output flow rates of 40 ml/hour) was used for all experiments... In the CSTR (200-ml culture volume)... Oxygen was sparged into the reactor at 3.4% of the gas feed
records.notes: CSTR (chemostat) with constant culture volume 200 mL and equal input/output flow of 40 mL/h, giving dilution rate D = F/V = 40 mL h-1 / 200 mL = 0.2 h-1 (paper independently states this corresponds to a residence time of 5 h). At true chemostat steady state, specific growth rate mu = D, so mu = 0.2 h-1 for Kp in the Kp-Bt coexistence steady states (sample points where Kp and Bt co-occur, glucose input swept 5->0 mM showing hysteresis). Doubling time = ln(2)/0.2 h-1 = 3.47 h (calculated). Oxygen sparged continuously (3.4% of 50 mL/min gas feed) but measured dissolved oxygen was near zero in the Kp-Bt state, i.e. functionally microaerobic/anoxic for this organism-pairing; classified microaerophilic because O2 flux was continuously supplied even though consumed. Medium composition taken from the 'Continuous culture of Kp and Bt' methods section; glucose was the swept variable across steady-state sample points S1-S8.

records.cite: Khazaei et al. Metabolic multistability and hysteresis in a model aerobe-anaerobe microbiome community. Science Advances (2020). DOI:10.1126/sciadv.ab
records.snip: at 5 mM glucose, a new distinct steady state was reached where Bt grew in the presence of Kp... we reduced the glucose input back down to 2, 1, 0.25, and 0 mM and found that the aerobe-anaerobe state persisted
records.notes: Same CSTR as the paired Kp record; D = F/V = 40 mL h-1 / 200 mL = 0.2 h-1 (residence time 5 h, explicitly stated). At chemostat steady state mu = D, so calculated mu = 0.2 h-1 for the obligate anaerobe Bt in the Kp-Bt coexistence state (Bt grows despite continuous O2 sparging because Kp consumes essentially all of it, keeping dissolved O2 near zero). Doubling time = ln(2)/0.2 h-1 = 3.47 h. Bt depends on Kp for oxygen removal and in turn supplies Kp with oligosaccharides from dextran breakdown (cross-feeding); classified anaerobic because Bt is an obligate anaerobe and measured dissolved O2 in this state was near zero.

records.med: M9 minimal medium + fumarate (post nutrient-shift from glucose)
records.cite: Radzikowski et al. Bacterial persistence is an active sigma S stress response to metabolic flux limitation. Molecular Systems Biology (2016). DOI:10.1
records.snip: After that, the persisters grew at a rate of 0.02 +/- 0.005 h-1 (95% confidence interval) (Fig 2B) and their volume remained constant
records.notes: Growth rate directly reported for the non-/slow-growing 'persister' subpopulation that emerges after an abrupt glucose-to-fumarate nutrient shift (0.02 +/- 0.005 h-1, 95% CI, from linear regression slope of log-transformed cell-count time course after the initial 0-2 h reductive-division period). Doubling time calculated as ln(2)/0.02 h-1 = 34.7 h (calculated from reported mu). Cultivated in M9 minimal medium supplemented with 2 g/L fumarate as sole carbon source, 37 C, aerobic shaking (300 rpm) batch culture in Erlenmeyer flasks; strain E. coli K12 BW25113 (MG1655 used for some model-validation experiments only). Note: fumarate uptake rate (FUR), oxygen transfer rate (OTR) and CO2 transfer rate (CTR) were also measured by the paper (RAMOS device / HPLC) but only qualitative/relative values (e.g. 'approximately one order of magnitude lower than fumarate-growing cells', yields 5.2x and 2.1x/1.3x different) are given in the main text; the underlying absolute mmol/gDW/h numbers are only in Appendix Table S2, which is not part of this XML, so no uptake/secretion rate was extracted per the no-invention rule.

records.cite: Imachi et al. Isolation of an archaeon at the prokaryote–eukaryote interface. Nature (2020). DOI:10.1038/s41586-019-1916-6. [PMC7015854]
records.snip: This approach gradually enriched the archaeon, which has an extremely slow growth rate and low cell yield...The culture consistently had a 30-60-day lag phase and required more than 3 months to reach full growth...The do
records.notes: Doubling time (14-25 days) reported directly in main text, estimated from a semi-logarithmic plot of qPCR-based 16S rRNA gene copy number data over time (per Methods: 'The doubling times of MK-D1 were calculated based on the semi-logarithmic plot of the qPCR data'). Converted to hours: 14 d x 24 = 336 h; 25 d x 24 = 600 h. growth_rate_per_h calculated as mu = ln2/doubling_time_h, giving a range of 0.00116 h^-1 (at 600 h doubling time) to 0.00206 h^-1 (at 336 h doubling time). No numeric substrate uptake or product secretion rates (in mass or molar per gDW per h) were given; amino acid utilization and H2/formate production were demonstrated qualitatively (NanoSIMS isotope tracing, depletion monitoring) without reported flux values.

records.snip: we grew MIT9301 at the higher irradiance (50 umol photons m-2 s-1) under semi-continuous phosphate limitation through daily transfers (at a dilution rate of 0.3 day-1) in media with 20-fold lower phosphate concentrations
records.notes: Dilution rate D = 0.3 day^-1 explicitly reported for the semi-continuous (daily-transfer), phosphate-limited culture of MIT9301 maintained for >50 generations at steady state. Converted to per-hour: 0.3 day^-1 / 24 = 0.0125 h^-1. growth_rate_per_h calculated assuming steady-state mu = D (standard assumption for balanced semi-continuous/chemostat-like culture held at fixed dilution for many generations). doubling_time_h calculated as ln2/mu = ln2/0.0125 = 55.5 h. No specific growth rate (mu) values for the other (non-phosphate-limited) batch cultures of MIT9301/MIT0801/MIT9313 are given in the main text (paper states 'growth rates provided in Table S1', which is a supplementary file not present in this XML, so not extracted). No substrate uptake or product secretion rates in mmol/gDW/h were reported; metabolite data are given as intracellular/extracellular concentrations per cell (attomoles/cell), not fluxes.

records.cite: Walker et al. Functional responses of methanogenic archaea to syntrophic growth. The ISME Journal (2012). DOI:10.1038/ismej.2012.60. [PMC3475374]

up.method: calculated: chemostat mass balance rate = D x (Sin - Sout); Sin=30 mM (feed lactate), Sout=4 mM (steady-state residual lactate), D=0.039 h^-1

sec.method: calculated: chemostat mass balance rate = D x (Pout - Pin); Pin assumed 0 mM (acetate-free feed medium), Pout=26 mM (steady-state acetate), D=0.039 h^-1

records.snip: a culture medium flow rate of 1.3 ml min-1 (25.6 h retention time) was initiated... the chemostat was assumed to be at steady state... Using the average concentrations measured during the steady-state growth experiments
records.notes: Chemostat coculture of D. vulgaris Hildenborough + M. maripaludis S2 in a 2-l working volume (3-l FairMenTec vessel) at 37C (310 K), dark, N2:CO2 headspace (anaerobic). Dilution rate D = flow rate / volume = 1.3 ml/min x 60 / 2000 ml = 0.039 h^-1, matching reported retention time of 25.6 h (D=1/25.6=0.0391 h^-1). At chemostat steady state, growth_rate_per_h (mu) = D. Volumetric lactate uptake and acetate secretion rates calculated via steady-state mass balance rate = D x delta-concentration, using feed lactate (30 mM), steady-state residual lactate (4 mM) and steady-state acetate (26 mM) reported in the paper; feed acetate assumed 0 mM (not stated explicitly, inferred since base CCM for coculture is described only as containing lactate, with acetate-substituted CCM reserved for a separate monoculture system). Units left volumetric (mmol/L/h) because no biomass concentration (gDW/L) was given in-text to convert to specific rate.

records.cite: Walker et al. Functional responses of methanogenic archaea to syntrophic growth. The ISME Journal (2012). DOI:10.1038/ismej.2012.60. [PMC3475374]
records.snip: a culture medium flow rate of 1.3 ml min-1 (25.6 h retention time) was initiated when the OD600 reached 0.325-0.350. The chemostat was assumed to be at steady state...
records.notes: Same chemostat coculture vessel as D. vulgaris record (M. maripaludis S2 growing syntrophically on H2 produced by D. vulgaris lactate fermentation, sulfate-free CCM, 37C, anaerobic). At chemostat steady state mu = D = flow rate/volume = 1.3 ml/min x 60/2000 ml = 0.039 h^-1 (consistent with reported 25.6 h retention time, D=1/25.6=0.0391 h^-1). H2 uptake and CH4 secretion by M. maripaludis were only reported as headspace partial pressures (Pa), not concentrations, so no uptake/secretion rate could be calculated without a Henry's-law conversion not provided in the paper.

up.units: mmol/day (bulk reactor, 2 L membrane bioreactor working volume)

records.snip: KHCO3 consumption indeed increased exponentially over time... we calculated the growth rate at pH 3.5 to reach 0.196 day-1, corresponding to a doubling time of 3.5 days.
records.notes: Growth rate mu=0.196 day-1 (doubling time 3.5 days) explicitly reported by authors, calculated from exponential increase in KHCO3 consumption (proxy for acid production during NH4+ oxidation) during initial 20-day batch phase of a 2 L continuous-flow membrane bioreactor fed 76.2 mmol NH4Cl/day. Converted to per-h: 0.196/24=0.008167 h-1; doubling time 3.5*24=84 h. Uptake rate: NH4+ consumed = fed (76.2 mmol/day) - not consumed (17.7 mmol/day) = 58.5 mmol/day (Table 1 nitrogen balance, chemostat/continuous-mode operation after switch from batch); NOT normalized per gDW (no biomass mass given), so reported as bulk reactor rate only, low-medium confidence. Secretion rates for NO2-, NO3-, NO/NO2 gas taken directly from Table 1 (values reported as mean +/- SD, n=3), also bulk reactor rates not gDW-normalized.

Converted to per-h: 0.104/24=0.004333 h-1; doubling time 6.7*24=160.8 h. Uptake rate: urea consumed = fed (31.1 mmol/day) - not consumed (27.7 mmol/day) = 3.4 mmol/day, measured over an 8 h period in the exponential phase of the batch reactor (Table 1); not gDW-normalized, bulk reactor rate only. Secretion rates for NO2-, NO3-, NO/NO2 gas taken directly from Table 1 (n=3).

records.notes: Doubling time (dt=5.33 h, 95% CI 5.28-5.38) reported directly in text for strain EC139ΔmecA complemented with mecA246G, grown in SSM9PR minimal medium + 16 µg/ml penicillin (Fig. 6d). Growth rate calculated as mu = ln2/dt = 0.6931/5.33 = 0.1300 h-1. Medium composition (SSM9PR) given in Methods: 1x M9 salts, 2 mM MgSO4, 0.1 mM CaCl2, 1% glucose, 1% casaminoacids, 1 mM Thiamine-HCl, 0.05 mM nicotinamide, 37°C, per ref 51 cited in paper.

records.cite: Song et al. Kinetics-based inference of environment-dependent microbial interactions and their dynamic variation. mSystems (2024). DOI:10.1128/msystem
records.snip: TABLE 1 ... μ1max (1/h) 2.961×10−1 ... K1− (g/L) 3.091×10−4 ... YS−/X1 (g/OD) 1.372 ... YS1+/X1 (mg/OD) 1.550 (X1 = ΔtrpC E. coli mutant, glucose = S−, tryptophan = S1+)
records.notes: mu1max=0.2961 h-1 is the maximal specific growth rate for X1 (ΔtrpC, JW1254-2) directly reported in Table 1 as a kinetic model parameter fit to batch/fed-batch experimental growth, glucose, and amino-acid time-course data (K3 minimal medium, glucose-limited, 37C, aerobic shake flask 225 rpm). doubling_time_h calculated = ln2/0.2961 = 2.34 h. Glucose uptake rate calculated as qS = mu * Y(S-/X1) = 0.2961 h-1 * 1.372 g/OD = 0.4064 g glucose/OD/h (paper's Y(S-/X1) is defined as g substrate consumed per unit OD of biomass, i.e. inverse of a conventional Yxs; units are OD-normalized, not gDW-normalized, because no OD-to-gDW conversion factor was given in this paper). Tryptophan secretion (cross-feeding product) calculated as qP = mu * Y(S1+/X1) = 0.2961 h-1 * 1.550 mg/OD = 0.459 mg/OD/h. Confidence medium: growth rate is a model-fit parameter (not a single raw time-course slope) and uptake/secretion units are OD- rather than gDW-normalized.

records.snip: The mixed population growth rate under turbidostat conditions in glucose was 0.94 doublings h−1, in glycerol the growth rate was 0.53 doublings h−1.
records.notes: Paper reports growth rate directly as 'doublings per hour' (=1/doubling_time), not natural-log mu. Converted: doubling_time_h = 1/0.94 = 1.064 h; growth_rate_per_h (mu) = ln(2) x 0.94 = 0.6515 h^-1 (rounded 0.65). This is the pooled/mixed sgRNA-library population growth rate in the turbidostat used to rescale the time axis for the whole experiment (M9 minimal media, pH 6.5, 0.4% glucose, 35 µg/ml kanamycin, 37°C, OD600 clamped at 0.15), not a single-gene knockdown value. A second passage in the text states '0.5 doublings h−1 in glycerol' (rounded) vs the more precise 0.53 given for glucose-condition sentence; used the 0.94/0.53 pair as primary reported values.

records.notes: Paper reports doubling time td=33 min (0.55 h) and elongation rate λ=0.021 min-1 for filamentous SulA-induced E. coli MG1655 cells recovering from confinement (Phase 2, unconfined), stated to be in agreement with bulk LB culture growth measurements. growth_rate_per_h = ln2/doubling_time_h = 0.693/0.55 = 1.26 h-1 (calculated from the reported td). Growth medium is LB per Methods (used throughout except where M63 minimal media noted for fluorescence experiments); exact incubation temperature for this specific λ measurement is not restated at that point in the text, but 37°C is the temperature used for the corresponding SulA-filamentation/imaging protocol described in Methods, so recorded with medium confidence. This reflects elongation/growth rate of filamentous (division-inhibited) cells, not a conventional balanced-growth batch culture, which further limits confidence for GEM-constraint use.

records.snip: We set the inflow of fresh medium to 0.116 ml h-1 per ml of culture, mimicking the severe nutrient starvation that limits Salmonella growth in mice (approximately 0.16 divisions per hour, generation time approximately 6
records.notes: Standard tissue-mimicking chemostat condition: dilution rate D=0.116 h-1 explicitly reported; at chemostat steady state mu=D. Doubling time = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.116 = 5.97 h, matching the paper's own stated 'generation time approximately 6 h'. Medium composition from Methods (100 mM MES, 5 mM KCl, 15 mM NH4Cl, 0.5 mM K2SO4, 1 mM KH2PO4, 50 uM MgSO4, 0.02% casamino acids, 0.02% glycerol, 0.0042% N-acetyl-glucosamine, 0.003% glucose, 0.0018% glucosamine, 0.005% histidine, 25 mM NaHCO3, pH 5.5) with constant 10% O2 / 5% CO2 gas stream.

records.snip: we grew Salmonella in low-density batch cultures, keeping all other parameters the same as in tissue-mimicking chemostats. Facile nutrient access under these conditions led to approximately tenfold faster replication (ar
records.notes: Paper reports 'around 1.5 divisions per hour' for batch (undiluted) culture. Using the paper's own consistent convention (division rate = 1/generation_time = mu/ln2, cross-validated against the chemostat statement where D=0.116 h-1 corresponds to '~0.16 divisions per hour' and generation time ~6h), mu = 1.5 x ln(2) = 1.04 h-1; doubling time = 1/1.5 = 0.67 h.

records.cite: Yadav et al. Organic matter degradation in the deep, sulfidic waters of the Black Sea: insights into the ecophysiology of novel anaerobic bacteria. Mi
records.snip: Clostridiales bacteria strains A1T and A2 grew rapidly (doubling time: 2–3 h) on cellobiose but twice as slow as on simpler carbon sources like glucose, while no growth occurred with pyruvate (Fig. S13).
records.notes: Doubling time (2-3 h) directly reported for strains A1T/A2 grown on cellobiose (medium BS6, cellobiose 0.2 g/L; BS-series composition given in Materials and Methods, pH 7.0 as prepared). growth_rate_per_h calculated as mu=ln2/td using td=2h and td=3h bounds -> 0.347 and 0.231 h-1. Strains are described as strictly anaerobic elsewhere in the paper. No growth occurred with pyruvate. The text also states cellobiose growth was 'twice as slow as on simpler carbon sources like glucose', but the sentence is ambiguous about direction (whether glucose growth is 2x faster or 2x slower); a numeric glucose rate was NOT recorded to avoid fabrication from an ambiguous sentence.

records.notes: Reported: morbidostat (continuous culture) run at dilution rate D=0.35 h-1, drug-free ancestral strain growth described as '0.5 doublings per hour'. doubling_time_h = 1/0.5 = 2.0 h (directly from reported doublings/h). growth_rate_per_h (specific rate mu) calculated as mu = (doublings/h) x ln2 = 0.5 x 0.6931 = 0.347 h-1 (close to, but not identical to, the operating dilution rate of 0.35 h-1, consistent with steady-state morbidostat operation targeting OD 0.15 in the absence of drug).

records.notes: Directly reported specific ethanol productivity for WT+pJS71 in ZRMG rich medium without hydrolysate, small-scale aerobic batch fermentation, 30C, shaking 200 rpm, fermentation started at 0.1 OD600. records.snip: The average thickness of bacterial microcolonies increased for the WT... Altogether, the WT strain grew with a doubling time of roughly 200 min whereas the mFabT strain did not grow.
records.notes: Doubling time (~200 min = 3.33 h) is explicitly reported in the text for WT GAS growing on human decidua tissue surface (live time-lapse imaging, flow conditions, 4 h observation window; ex vivo assay run at 37°C + 5% CO2 per Methods). growth_rate_per_h calculated as mu = ln(2)/doubling_time_h = 0.6931/3.333 = 0.208 h^-1 (rounded to 0.21). Medium is host tissue (human decidua), not a defined chemical medium, so no medium_composition could be extracted; RPMI (control, no defined recipe given) did not support growth. Confidence set to medium because 'roughly 200 min' is an approximate value and the growth substrate is complex host tissue rather than a controlled defined medium. The isogenic mFabT (FabT^H105Y) mutant strain did not grow under this condition (no measurable rate).

up.method: calculated: qS = mu/Yxs = (1 h^-1)/(88 g biomass per mol glucose) = 0.01136 mol/(g*h)

sec.method: calculated: qP = qS(glucose) x Y(acetate/glucose) = 11.36 mmol/gDW/h x 2 mol acetate/mol glucose

records.snip: Growth rate on glucose (mu_g) 1 h-1 [Si et al. (2017)]; Biomass yield on glucose 88 g/mol [Holms (1996)]; Acetate yield on glucose 2 mol/mol [Holms (1996)] (Table 1).
records.notes: Table 1 of this paper lists model parameters mu_glucose=1 h^-1, biomass yield on glucose=88 g/mol, and acetate yield on glucose=2 mol/mol, explicitly attributed by the authors to Si et al. (2017) and Holms (1996) respectively -- i.e. these are literature-derived parameters reused for this paper's mathematical model of biphasic growth, NOT primary experimental measurements made in this specific study (which itself only reports relative growth-rate changes, e.g. '39% higher' for evolved strain IR1, without an absolute mu). qS(glucose) and qP(acetate) were calculated here from the explicit numbers stated in this paper's Table 1 using qS=mu/Yxs and qP=qS*Yps. Confidence low because organism/strain and exact medium composition are not specified for this parameter set, and the values are secondary citations reproduced in this paper rather than its own experiment.

records.notes: Not a lab culture: field/in-situ estimate for an uncultured natural archaeal population. Paper's calculation chain: (1) ammonia-oxidation (AO) rate measured by 15NH4Cl tracer incubation (96 h, 0C, dark); (2) chemoautotrophic C fixation = AO rate / 10 (10 mol NH4 oxidized per mol C fixed, cited from Berg et al. 2015); (3) new Thaumarchaeota cell production = AO rate x (46.6-52.4 cells produced per nmol ammonium oxidized, mean 49.5, from Konneke et al. 2005 and Martens-Habbena et al. 2009); (4) population growth rate = new-cell-production-rate / standing Thaumarchaeota abundance (from qPCR of amoA or rrs gene copies). We converted the paper's reported per-day growth rate (mean 0.50/d using amoA-based abundance; range 0.01-2.5/d) to per-hour by dividing by 24 (0.50/24=0.0208 h-1; range 0.01/24-2.5/24 = 0.00042-0.104 h-1). Values are averages across all seasons, depths and water masses combined (AASW/WW/CDW). No defined growth medium exists (natural seawater); organism not culturable/strain-resolved. Growth-rate range: 0.01-2.5 per day (amoA-based).

records.notes: Same calculation chain as the amoA-based record in this file, but here Thaumarchaeota standing abundance was estimated from rrs (16S rRNA) gene qPCR instead of amoA. Paper-reported mean = 0.31 per day (range 0.01-2.1 per day), converted to per hour by dividing by 24: 0.31/24=0.0129 h-1; range 0.01/24-2.1/24 = 0.00042-0.0875 h-1. Same caveats as above record (uncultured natural population, no defined medium, averaged across seasons/water masses).

records.notes: Season-resolved subset of the same amoA-based population-growth-rate calculation described above (AO-rate-derived cell production / amoA-based Thaumarchaeota abundance). A companion summer value (0.26 per day = 0.0108 h-1) is reported in the paper as significantly lower (P<0.0001) but is not separately entered here to avoid over-fragmenting near-duplicate rows; see source_snippet for both numbers verbatim.

Medium (BHI+2.5% NaCl, 37C, aerobic) is the growth condition used elsewhere in this paper's own methods (growth-inhibition assay / mid-log harvest) for V. natriegens ATCC14048, but the 10-min doubling time itself is not tied to a specific medium/composition measurement in this paper.

records.cite: D’Souza et al. Nutrient complexity triggers transitions between solitary and colonial growth in bacterial populations. The ISME Journal (2021). DOI:10
records.snip: ...but took 15.50 ± 7.55 h to double in xylose (mean ± 95% CI, Fig. 2c)
records.notes: Paper reports population-doubling time of 15.50 ± 7.55 h (mean ± 95% CI, n=9 chambers) for C. crescentus CB15 on 0.05% xylose in M2 minimal medium within microfluidic chambers (30C). mu = ln2/15.50 h = 0.0447 h^-1 (calculated by curator). This is the dispersing/solitary-growth condition (cells do not aggregate on xylose); note this doubling time reflects sessile-cell accumulation in the chamber, which is lower than single-cell growth rate because swarmer daughter cells disperse away rather than because of slow division per se (paper notes similar per-cell division propensity in both substrates).

records.strain: N/A (whole fecal community from 5 human donors, #1,#2,#8,#9,#10)
records.med: sterile growth medium in chemostat vessel (composition not detailed in main text; designed to mimic human distal colon per cited ref [30])
records.cite: Santiago-Rodriguez et al. Chemostat culture systems support diverse bacteriophage communities from human feces. Microbiome (2015). DOI:10.1186/s40168-
records.snip: adding 100 mL into 300 mL of sterile growth medium in each vessel. The pH within each vessel was then adjusted to 6.9–7.0...maintained at 37 °C...the retention rate set to 16.67 mL/h with...sparging of O2 free N2 gas to
records.notes: Community-level chemostat, not a single defined species/strain (this is the main reason confidence is low for GEM-constraint purposes). Working volume = 100 mL inoculum + 300 mL medium = 400 mL; flow (retention) rate = 16.67 mL/h. Dilution rate D = Q/V = 16.67/400 = 0.0417 h^-1 (calculated by curator from these two explicit numbers). At chemostat steady state, mu = D = 0.0417 h^-1; doubling_time = ln2/0.0417 = 16.6 h (calculated). pH range 6.9-7.0 reported as adjusted set point (midpoint 6.95 used here); anaerobic atmosphere (90% N2/5% CO2/5% H2) confirmed elsewhere in Methods.

records.notes: In vivo growth rate of S. Typhimurium SL1344 in mouse cecal lymph node (cLN) during the first day post-infection, before ciprofloxacin treatment. Paper states r=2.82/day and doubling time=5.9 h directly (both reported). mu = 2.82/24 h = 0.1175 h^-1 (curator cross-check, consistent with ln2/5.9h=0.1175 h^-1). Not a defined lab medium - this is a host-tissue (in vivo) measurement from a stochastic birth-immigration model fit to WITS-tagged strain counts.

records.notes: Doubling time (40 min = 0.667 h) is stated explicitly in this paper (citing Neidhardt et al 1990, i.e. a secondary/borrowed value used as the biomass-composition basis for iAF1260, not new primary data generated by Feist et al). growth_rate_per_h calculated as mu = ln2 / doubling_time_h = 0.6931/0.667 = 1.04 h-1. records.cite: Pester et al. A 'rare biosphere' microorganism contributes to sulfate reduction in a peatland. The ISME Journal (2010). DOI:10.1038/ismej.2010.75. [PM
records.snip: we estimated the cell-specific sulfate reduction rate (cs-SRR) for this Desulfosporosinus sp. to be 341 fmol SO4 2− cell−1 day−1. The rate was calculated by dividing the measured SRR of the turnover experiments [26.2 nmo
records.notes: Cell-specific sulfate reduction (substrate uptake) rate is explicitly calculated in the paper: cs-SRR = measured bulk SRR [26.2 nmol SO4^2- (g soil wet wt)-1 day-1, from stable-isotope-probing turnover incubations at 14°C, pH 4, anoxic] divided by the natural Desulfosporosinus population size (from qPCR 16S rRNA gene abundance / 4.4 rRNA-gene copies per cell). Not a pure-culture growth-medium experiment (uncultured environmental population in soil slurry), so no growth_rate could be determined; units left as reported (fmol/cell/day) since no dry-weight conversion factor is given in the paper.

records.snip: A stable population of a photosynthetic GSB, Prosthecochloris phaeobacteroides BS1, lives there at a depth of ∼100 to 110 m and has an estimated in situ doubling time of ≥2.8 years (Overmann and Pfennig 1992) or more
records.notes: Doubling time (≥2.8 years = 2.8*365*24 ≈ 24,528 h) is stated explicitly in this review's text but is a secondary citation of Overmann and Pfennig (1992), not primary data generated by this paper. Reflects an in situ natural chemocline population (Black Sea), not a defined lab medium/culture — no growth_rate_per_h computed because the doubling time is a lower-bound estimate ("≥"), so mu=ln2/td would misrepresent it as exact; recorded as doubling_time_h only. Confidence low given secondary-source origin and open-ended (≥) value.
